posts.summary: Charity Majors introduces her new blog, outlining topics she plans to write about including tech hiring practices, infrastructure tooling, and startup acquisitions. The post is a lighthearted launch announcement that also explains the origin of her online handle '@mipsytipsy'.
key_points: Plans to write about broken tech hiring practices
Wants a less ephemeral platform than Twitter for sharing ideas
Interested in infrastructure tooling proliferation and startup acquisition survival
money_quotes: The tech industry is terrible at interviewing and hiring, and some ideas for making it slightly less terrible.
The appalling proliferation of new ways to bootstrap your infrastructure over the past couple years.
"Start a blog" has been on my todo list for so long, it's going to leave a gaping void in my life when I check it off.
posts.key_insight: This introductory post previews the themes that would define Charity's writing career: fixing broken tech hiring, making sense of infrastructure chaos, and navigating startup life.

posts.summary: Charity Majors recaps her 2015 highlights: speaking at 21 conferences while restless as a manager, championing MongoDB's maturation through the RocksDB storage engine integration at Parse, and leaving Parse/Facebook after 3.5 years when comfort became stagnation.
key_points: Spoke at 21 conferences in one year, driven by restlessness and the fragmented calendar of being a manager at a big company
Led Parse as the alpha customer for MongoRocks, achieving 1/10th storage space and 50-200x faster writes
Left Parse/Facebook after 3.5 years when the 3-4 hour commute and lack of growth became untenable
Argues 2015 was MongoDB's 'InnoDB moment' when it became a real database through storage engine diversity
money_quotes: Being a manager at a big company makes this depressing Swiss cheese out of your calendar. You can't really carve out the heads-down time you would need in order to do challenging technical work.

There is nothing as heady as getting to pour yourself into a product that people are passionate about, tackle problems that are hard and might actually be impossible, with a team that blows your socks off with their brilliance and joy and hilariousness on a daily basis.
I need a certain amount of chaos and panic in my life. Being comfortable for too long just makes me miserable.
Hey guess what, software actually can get better!
posts.key_insight: Growth requires leaving comfort behind — whether that means a database outgrowing its storage engine or an engineer outgrowing a role that no longer challenges them.

posts.summary: Charity Majors reflects on the emotional and professional reality of being acquired by a large company (Facebook's acquisition of Parse), offering hard-won advice for startup founders and engineers going through the same experience. She emphasizes that trauma is normal, your product and customers are no longer yours, and you must either adapt to the new corporate culture or leave.
key_points: Acquisitions are always massively disruptive, even the 'good' ones — trauma is the norm
Your product becomes 'strategic alignment' and your customer becomes your corporate overlord
The acquiring company's culture will dominate; you must learn its tribal and hierarchical games to succeed
If you can't embrace the new parent company, leave sooner rather than later — unless golden handcuffs apply, in which case emotionally disengage
Aggressively assume good intent from the acquirer, even when they condescend to you
money_quotes: Trauma is the norm. Acquisitions are always massively disruptive and upsetting, even the good ones.
Your product is now 'strategic alignment'. Look at yourself in the mirror and repeat that five times every morning.
Aggressively assume good intent. If a big company buys up your tiny startup, lots of people are going to condescend to you and assume they know how to solve your own problems better than you do.
If you honestly can't see yourself ever embracing the new parent company, whether for cultural or ethical or technical reasons, you should leave sooner rather than later.
posts.key_insight: Surviving an acquisition means accepting that your product, customers, and culture now belong to someone else — and deciding honestly whether you can live with that or need to walk away.

posts.summary: Charity Majors shares her brutally honest experience adopting Terraform for infrastructure orchestration, documenting the progression from initial delight through debugging hell with modules and inscrutable error messages. She concludes that Terraform is best-in-class for infrastructure orchestration but is still too immature for high-reliability production use, recommending it only for defining static infrastructure bones rather than managing dynamic resources.
key_points: Terraform is best in class for infrastructure orchestration despite being immature
Modules are like functions (variables are arguments, outputs are return values) but debugging them is nightmarish
Terraform should only manage static infrastructure bones (networking, NAT, ASGs), not dynamic data or service registries
Terraform doesn't play nicely with existing infrastructure - it wants to own everything
CloudFormation's lack of opinionation and missing plan preview make it inferior despite Terraform's rough edges
money_quotes: Because I am fucking sick and tired of not having versioned infrastructure. Jesus christ, the ways my teams have bent over backwards to fake infra versioning after the fact.

There is precious little evidence that TF was developed by or for anyone with experience running production systems at scale, but it's definitely not as actively hostile as CloudFormation, so it's got that going for it.
Terraform is fantastic for defining the bones of your infrastructure. Your networking, your NAT, autoscaling groups, the bits that are robust and rarely change.

I would rather adopt one opinionated philosophy for my infrastructure, supplementing where necessary, than duct tape together fifty different half baked philosophies about how software and infrastructure should work and spend all my time mediating their conflicts.
AWS has no opinions, only slobbering, squidlike, directionless-flopping optionalities.
posts.key_insight: Terraform is the best available option for versioned infrastructure orchestration, but its immaturity means you should constrain it to static infrastructure bones and never let it manage dynamic resources in high-reliability production environments.

posts.summary: Charity Majors compares three AWS networking patterns for managing multiple environments (flat network, account-per-env, VPC-per-env) and argues strongly for the VPC-per-environment model. She debunks outdated beliefs about VPC limitations and makes the case that VPCs enable treating infrastructure as testable, promotable code rather than duct-taped configurations.
key_points: Three common AWS environment patterns: flat network with security groups, one account per environment, and one VPC per environment
Many engineers hold outdated beliefs about AWS VPC limits — the hard cap is now 200, not 5 or 10
VPC-per-environment enables testing infrastructure changes (like Terraform modules) on staging before promoting to production
The account-per-environment model provides strong security isolation but comes with painful billing complexity and operational overhead
Infrastructure as code isn't real unless you can spin up a full copy of your infra and test it
money_quotes: If you can't spin up a full copy of your infra and test it, you don't actually have 'infrastructure as code.' You just have … some code, and duct tape.

VPC is the future and it is awesome, and unless you have some VERY SPECIFIC AND CONVINCING reasons to do otherwise, you should be spinning up a VPC per environment with orchestration and prob doing it from CI on every code commit, almost like it's just like, you know, code.
AWS billing is ALREADY a horrendous hairball coughed up by the world's ugliest cat, I can't even imagine trying to wrangle multiple accounts.

VPCs are here to help you think about infrastructure like regular old code. Lots of VPCs are approximately as easy to manage as one VPC. Unlike lots of accounts, which are there to give you headaches and one-offs and custom scripts and pointy-clicky shit and complicated horrible things to work around.
posts.key_insight: VPC-per-environment is the best AWS networking pattern because it gives you real isolation while enabling you to treat infrastructure as testable, promotable code — which is the only way 'infrastructure as code' actually means anything.

posts.summary: After a catastrophic Terraform outage caused by a poisoned shared state file — triggered by simple staging changes that took down all environments — Charity Majors walks through the painful debugging and recovery process, then argues forcefully for using separate Terraform state files per environment to limit blast radius.
key_points: A simple staging change poisoned a shared Terraform state file, causing crashes across all environments and requiring manual deletion of all infrastructure three times
When Terraform crashes, find the 'panic' in the crash log — the error messages at the end of the run are red herrings
Applying individual Terraform modules can silently create duplicate AWS resources like VPCs and Route53 zones
Separate state files per environment are essential to limit blast radius and enable safe testing in isolation
The terraform_remote_state resource enables querying values across state files, making multi-state architectures clean and practical
money_quotes: If you can't safely test your changes in isolation away from prod, you don't have infrastructure as code.
It is still as green as the motherfucking Shire and you should assume that every change you make could destroy the world.
I ended up deleting my entire infrastructure by hand, three times.
This whole thing started when I made some simple changes to staging. To STAGING.
Your job as a responsible engineer is to add guard rails, build a clear promotion path for validating changesets into production, and limit the scope of the world it is capable of destroying.
posts.key_insight: Sharing a single Terraform state file across environments means any change to staging can destroy production — separate state files per environment are the minimum viable safety net for infrastructure as code.

posts.summary: Charity Majors evangelizes nail polish as the ultimate paint for personalizing tech equipment like keyboards, laptops, and monitors. She showcases her tradition of painting a new keyboard at every job and shares practical tips for using nail polish on electronics.
key_points: Nail polish is extremely durable - surviving years of daily use on keyboards and laptops with minimal wear
Charity has a tradition of painting a new keyboard every time she starts a new job
Cheap nail polish works fine; opacity matters more than brand
A reflective base coat (like silver chrome) is essential for bright colors on dark surfaces
money_quotes: Nail polish is the toughest, brightest, sparkliest, most durable paint in the world.
The idea of typing on a plain beige or black keyboard all day long is … actually one of the more depressing things I can imagine.
Yo, this shit can bang around in my laptop bag for years and never chip or wear off or fade. Nail polish is the most badass paint in the entire world.
posts.key_insight: Nail polish is a surprisingly durable and accessible way to personalize your tech gear, lasting years of daily abuse without chipping or fading.

posts.summary: A practical brain dump of hard-won Terraform and AWS networking tips covering security groups, VPC peering, NAT gateways, route tables, and other infrastructure-as-code gotchas. Charity shares specific configurations and debugging strategies learned through painful trial and error, emphasizing the unintuitive nature of AWS networking when managed through Terraform.
key_points: Security groups are stackable in VPC and are often the root cause of networking issues, not routes or VPC config
AWS resource description fields are not comments — changing them destroys and recreates resources
VPC peering must be set up bidirectionally before connections work in either direction
NAT gateways attach to public subnets (not private), with routes from private subnets pointing to them
Use AWS Certificate Manager instead of managing your own certs, and use NAT gateways instead of custom Linux NAT boxes
money_quotes: Since you have no ability to debug AWS networking via normal linux utilities, my best debugging tip for VPC networking is still, when you get really stuck open up all the SG ports and see if that fixes it.
BUT MIND THE FUCKING TRAP. You do not attach these NAT gateways to your PRIVATE subnets, you attach them to the PUBLIC FUCKING SUBNETS, and then a route from the private subnet to that gateway.
Yesterday I spun up another whole new VPC clone by adding about 5 lines and copying a couple files + sed -e'ing the name of the environment. Took about two minutes, felt like a fucking badass.
I will now proceed to forget as much as possible about all the things I have learned about networking over the past two months.
posts.key_insight: AWS networking via Terraform is full of unintuitive gotchas that aren't documented well, but once you've fought through the pain the payoff of reproducible infrastructure is enormous.

posts.summary: As serverless and SaaS abstractions proliferate, engineering teams can outsource labor but cannot outsource caring about operational concerns. You still own reliability, debuggability, and storage even when you don't run the infrastructure, so application engineers must develop operations-minded thinking about their core differentiators.
key_points: Outsourcing infrastructure doesn't absolve you of operational responsibility — you chose the provider, it's on you
Focus operational investment on the critical path that affects users, not internal tooling
Serverless and SaaS trade control and visibility for cost and specialization benefits
Stateful services remain a deep problem that serverless abstractions mostly ignore
The era of dedicated ops teams as pain-absorption buffers is ending in favor of engineers owning services end to end
money_quotes: You can outsource labor, but you can't outsource caring.
You chose them, it's on you. It's tacky to blame the software or the service, and besides your customers don't give a shit whose "fault" it is.
There's a server somewhere under all those abstractions, don't forget.
Software quality improves when feedback loops are tighter for software engineers, which means being on call and owning services end to end.
Figure out what your core differentiators are, and own the shit out of those.
posts.key_insight: Serverless and SaaS abstractions are inevitable and largely positive, but they shift — not eliminate — operational responsibility, requiring application engineers to think deeply about reliability, debuggability, and their core differentiators.

posts.summary: Charity Majors argues that operations is not a team or a role but an emergent property of how organizations ship and maintain quality software, and that the serverless/#NoOps movement dangerously devalues critical operational skills. Drawing on her experience building Parse's serverless infrastructure, she contends that no matter how elegant the abstractions, engineers still need to care about persistent state, reliability, and operational excellence.
key_points: Operations is an emergent organizational property, not a team responsibility
Serverless abstractions don't eliminate the need for operational skills
The #NoOps movement dangerously devalues critical engineering disciplines
Operations should be reclaimed as a term of pride, not rebranded away
The best software engineers are those who value deployment, instrumentation, and observability
money_quotes: No matter how pretty the abstractions are, you're still dealing with dusty old concepts like 'persistent state' and 'queries' and 'unavailability.'
Thinking about operational quality in terms of 'a thing some other team is responsible for' is just generally not associated with great outcomes.
You don't make operational outcomes magically better by renaming the team 'DevOps' or 'SRE' or anything else.
Some of the most creative cultural and technical changes in the technical landscape are being driven by the teams most identified with operations and developer tooling.
It's time to bring back 'operations' as a term of pride.
posts.key_insight: Operational excellence is an emergent property that every engineer must own — no amount of abstraction, rebranding, or outsourcing to a dedicated team eliminates the need for operational skills and values.

posts.summary: Charity Majors argues that the DevOps vs SRE debate is largely a failure to appreciate context — Google's SRE practices are excellent for Google but shouldn't be blindly applied elsewhere. She contends that DevOps philosophy is more adaptable and crowd-sourced than Google's prescriptive SRE model, and criticizes Google's insularity and apparent unawareness of the broader industry's decade of cultural transformation around operations.
key_points: The DevOps vs SRE debate stems from failing to recognize the overwhelming power of context — what works at Google scale doesn't automatically apply elsewhere
Google's SRE book contains great wisdom but comes across as tone-deaf and unaware of the broader DevOps movement's decade of progress
Operational excellence is everyone's responsibility, from support staff to the CEO
DevOps philosophy is more crowd-sourced, flexible, and adaptable than SRE because it emerged from a broader, more diverse collaborative base
Specialization is valuable but becomes harmful when it turns into compartmentalization or when large companies mistake their contextual solutions for universal truths
money_quotes: A lot of the philosophical volleying between devops / SRE comes down to a failure to recognize the overwhelming power of context.
If you try and just apply Google SRE principles to your own org according to their prescriptive model, you're gonna be in for a really, really bad time.
Compared with the SRE manifesto, the DevOps philosophy is much more crowd-sourced, more flexible, and adaptable to organizations of all stages of developments.

Large companies tend to get snobby and forget this. They stop hiring for unique strengths and start hiring for lack of weaknesses or 'Excellence in Whiteboard Coding Techniques,' and congratulate themselves alot about being The Best.
So many of us have had this experience of hiring ex-Googlers who automatically assume that the way Google does a thing is CORRECT, not just contextually appropriate.
posts.key_insight: The DevOps vs SRE debate is fundamentally a context problem — Google's SRE practices are contextually brilliant but the industry needs the more adaptable, crowd-sourced DevOps philosophy because one-size-fits-all solutions don't exist.

posts.summary: Charity Majors dissects a MongoDB outage caused by CleverTap's reckless storage engine migration, arguing that the real problem wasn't MongoDB but engineers treating stateful database services with the same cavalier attitude they use for stateless services. She emphasizes that managing databases requires paranoia, careful planning, and months of testing—not weekend upgrades—and urges 'accidental DBAs' to invest in learning proper data management practices.
key_points: Stateful services like databases require a fundamentally different operational mindset than stateless services
Storage engine migrations should be done incrementally with months of testing against real production workloads
Engineers who blame database vendors for their own operational failures are missing the point
The 'accidental DBA' role is increasingly common and requires deliberate investment in learning
The long-term aspiration should be making stateful services as operationally simple as stateless ones
money_quotes: Managing stateful services like databases means that you need to be more paranoid than you did with stateless services.
You can't just go 'dudes it's faster' and jump off a cliff. This shit is basic.
Don't blame the vendors when you fucked up.
The only cost is in dollars, which is fucking cheap compared to data or engineering time.
But I can criticize Mongo all day long. You children on hacker news who have never run it don't get to.
posts.key_insight: Database operations demand paranoia and patience—treating stateful services with the same move-fast-and-break-things attitude used for stateless services is a recipe for catastrophic, often unrecoverable data loss.

posts.summary: Charity Majors argues that engineers should reject the false choice between management and individual contributor tracks, instead embracing a pendulum approach of switching back and forth between the two roles. She contends that management is not a promotion but a lateral career change, and that the best technical leaders are those who have spent time in both roles, gaining complementary skills that make them uniquely effective.
key_points: The best engineering managers are never more than 2-3 years removed from hands-on technical work
Management is a lateral move to a parallel track, not a promotion — treating it as a promotion traps people in roles they hate
Engineering and management require opposite working styles (deep focus vs. constant availability) and cannot be done well simultaneously
Tech leads who have done time in management are more effective because they understand how to communicate, motivate, and get things done through people
The pendulum approach — alternating between IC and management roles — builds rare breadth and strength
money_quotes: The best frontline eng managers in the world are the ones that are never more than 2-3 years removed from hands-on work, full time down in the trenches. The best individual contributors are the ones who have done time in management.

Management is not a promotion, management is a change of profession. And you will be bad at it for a long time after you start doing it. If you don't think you're bad at it, you aren't doing your job.
Management is highly interruptive, and great engineering — where you're learning things — requires blocking out interruptions. You can't do these two opposite things at once.
Managing because it feeds your ego is a terrific way to be sure that your engineers get to report to someone miserable and resentful, someone who should really be writing code.
It isn't a promotion, so you don't have any status to give up. Do it as long as it makes you happy, and the people around you happy. Then stop. Go back to building things.
posts.key_insight: The most effective technical leaders treat management and engineering as a pendulum rather than a one-way promotion, building rare and complementary skills by intentionally switching between the two roles throughout their career.

posts.summary: Charity Majors describes her journey from deep cynicism about corporate values statements to reluctantly crafting one for Honeycomb, driven by top candidates consistently asking about company values. She argues that good values must be particular to the company, actionable for decision-making, and pithy enough to actually be used — not generic aspirational fluff.
key_points: Corporate values are usually disingenuous marketing, but good candidates kept asking about them, forcing Charity to take them seriously
Effective values should be particular to the company, help resolve conflicts and make decisions, and be memorable
Values that try to be universal end up meaning nothing — specificity elicits passion in a way that generic never can
Good values are living documents with known flaws, not permanent sacred texts
Values should reflect what the team already says to each other, not aspirational fiction
money_quotes: At best it's a disingenuous marketing campaign, usually it's more like a red blaring light shining on their degenerate hypocrisies and weakest aspirations.

If a place is for everyone, it is not for anyone in particular. I am okay with being a particular place, for particular people at a particular time in our lives. Specificity elicits passion in a way that generic never can.

Our values should be particular (they should not apply just as well to any other company), they should help make decisions and resolve conflict (if they aren't useful/if we don't use them then what's the point), they should be pithy and a bit snarky.
I even love that there are huge, known flaws with it because that reminds me this is a living document, that we have committed to its care and feeding and regular revisioning.
posts.key_insight: Company values are only worth having if they're specific enough to your company that someone might read them and think 'this place is not for me' — because values that apply to everyone are meaningless.

posts.summary: Charity Majors offers blunt, pragmatic career advice for women in tech: toughen up early, stay technical to build undeniable credibility, and then use your seniority to advocate for others. She argues against leaving tech entirely, instead urging people to quit bad companies rather than the industry itself.
key_points: Early career requires endurance — focus on leveling up skills and negotiating for more money and power
Technical excellence is the most durable form of credibility, especially when your competence gets questioned repeatedly
Senior contributors should use their power to advocate for others, exploiting the research-backed loophole that women aren't punished for advocating on behalf of others
Don't quit tech, quit your job — bad companies don't deserve your talent
The rest of the world is at least as sexist as tech but without the compensation and opportunities
money_quotes: Technical excellence is currency in this industry, even more so if your credibility is gonna get challenged again and again. So don't stop engineering til you're great at it.
Don't quit tech: quit your job. There are LOTS of tolerable-to-great companies out there. If you stay and suffer, you're just rewarding the shitholes with your presence.
Learn shit, save your money, amass great power. Then use it to fuck shit up.
The research convincingly shows that women get punished for advocating for themselves, but not for advocating for others. It's a sweet loophole, use it.
posts.key_insight: Build technical credibility and financial power first, then leverage your seniority to change the system from a position of strength rather than vulnerability.

posts.summary: Charity Majors post-mortems the backlash to her advice post telling women in tech to stay technical, earn power, and then use it to advocate for others. She walks through what feedback was valid (sloppy framing around gender identity, insufficient defensive writing) versus what was bad-faith pile-on, ultimately standing by her core advice while acknowledging specific communication failures.
key_points: Her original advice: tough it out as a junior, stay technical, then use your senior power to advocate for others
Valid criticism included not writing defensively for hostile readers and being sloppy about her own gender identity framing
She distinguishes between genuine feedback from trusted friends and performative outrage from internet mobs
The private response was overwhelmingly positive (100-1), especially from junior women, while public discourse was dominated by criticism
She frames the tension as between people who fight to succeed within a flawed system versus those who refuse to participate in it
money_quotes: We are far too chickenshit about telling young women straight up how to succeed at work. We praise them for all kinds of dumb shit and second shift work and emotional labor that has little if any strategic impact to the bottom line, and wonder why they're burned out and resentful.
I have no problem admitting my errors and making amends. I do it all the fucking time. But I am disinclined to grovel before a howling mob.
I'm tired of seeing women burn themselves out on shitty corporate diversity work that only diverts their energy from amassing real power and strategic impact.
I don't want a fucking consolation prize. I want to sweep the competition, I want to change the world. I can't be the only one who hungers for power, money and credibility.

There has always been tension between the people who see the world as it is and fight to succeed in it, and the people who opt out and refuse to participate because it's compromised. The world needs us both.
posts.key_insight: Honest, strategic career advice for women in tech — stay technical, accumulate real power, then use it — is more empowering than the corporate diversity messaging that diverts energy from building actual influence.

posts.summary: Power naturally drifts toward engineering managers over time, which can demoralize engineers and cause them to disengage or leave. To counter this, organizations should explicitly enumerate engineers' rights — autonomy, technical decision-making authority, career investment, and transparent feedback — alongside clear responsibilities for both engineers and managers.
key_points: Power flows toward managers naturally, like water downhill, and must be actively counterbalanced
When all power drifts to managers, people become managers for the wrong reasons and engineers disengage
Technical decisions must remain the provenance of engineers, not managers
Engineers deserve investment in leadership skills equal to what managers receive
Managers should be visualized as support structure below their teams, not perched atop
money_quotes: Power has a way of flowing towards people managers over time, no matter how many times you repeat 'management is not a promotion, it's a career change.'

But if you let all the power drift over to the engineering managers, pretty soon it doesn't look so great to be an engineer. Now you have people becoming managers for all the wrong reasons, or everyone saying they want to be a manager, or engineers just tuning out and turning in their homework (or quitting).
Technical decisions must remain the provenance of engineers, not managers.
I find it helpful to practice visualizing the org chart hierarchies upside down — placing managers below their teams as support structure rather than perched atop.
posts.key_insight: Organizations must actively codify and protect engineers' autonomy, technical authority, and career investment because power will naturally and silently concentrate in management unless structural counterweights are deliberately maintained.

posts.summary: Engineers possess far more latent power and influence than they typically realize, rooted in their unique ability to create and build. Rather than seeking management roles to gain influence, engineers should recognize and cultivate their existing forms of power — technical expertise, social skills, creative output — and deploy them strategically through communication and selective engagement.
key_points: Creation is the fundamental engineering superpower from which all other forms of influence flow
Engineers who want to become managers solely for power and influence are the worst candidates for management
Influence must be actively wielded through communication to become real power
Authoritarian power is brittle in creative work; sustainable power comes from soft influence
Influence should be spent strategically like innovation tokens, not spread thin across everything
money_quotes: Creation is the ur-power from which all our forms of power flow. Nothing gets built unless we agree to build it (which makes this an ethical issue, too).
Because engineering is a creative activity, authoritarian power is actually quite brittle and damaging. The only sustainable forms of power are so-called 'soft powers' like influencing and inspiring.
In the same way that submissives have ultimate power in healthy BDSM relationships, engineers actually have the ultimate power in healthy teams. You have the ultimate veto: you can refuse to create.
If you don't speak up, you don't have the right to sit and fume over your lack of influence. And speaking up does mean being vulnerable — and sometimes wrong — in front of other people.
People who want to be managers in order to perpetuate a bad power structure are the last people you want to be managers.
posts.key_insight: Engineers already hold the ultimate power — the power to create or refuse to create — but must actively communicate and strategically deploy their influence rather than ceding it to management by default.

posts.summary: Charity Majors argues that shipping software should not be a scary, high-risk event, and that organizations must invest in making their deploy pipelines resilient to failure rather than trying to eliminate errors entirely. She advocates for software ownership where every engineer who writes code also deploys, monitors, and debugs it in production, creating shorter feedback loops and better software. The path forward involves treating deploy tooling as first-class infrastructure, embracing failure as normal, and incrementally gaining confidence in releases through canaries, feature flags, and observability.
key_points: Deploy software is the most important software you have and should be treated as a first-class citizen with dedicated ownership and investment
Software ownership — writing, deploying, and debugging your own code — is the natural end state of DevOps
Shipping to production is about incrementally gaining confidence through canaries, feature flags, and observability, not flipping a switch
Scary deploys are self-reinforcing: the less often you ship, the scarier and riskier each deploy becomes
Operability should be a prerequisite for seniority — never promote someone who can't deploy and debug their own code
money_quotes: Deploy software is the most important software you have.
If shipping changes is scary and fraught, people will do it less and it will get even MORE scary and treacherous.
Software ownership is the natural end state of DevOps.
Nothing is production except production. Don't rely on never failing; expect failure, embrace failure. Practice failure!
Never promote someone to 'senior engineer' if they can't deploy and debug their own code.
posts.key_insight: Making deploys safe isn't about preventing all failures — it's about building resilient pipelines, embracing software ownership, and creating a culture where failures are small, frequent, non-events that everyone knows how to detect and recover from.

posts.summary: Charity Majors argues that the most effective way to develop leadership skills is not through books, coaches, or mentors, but through peer relationships where leaders swap stories and support each other. She describes how she built small peer groups that met regularly to practice vulnerability and share real, contextual advice, and publishes her framework for others to replicate.
key_points: Stories from peers are more useful than abstract management books because they are real, specific, and can be interrogated for context
Peer relationships are preferable to mentor-mentee hierarchies because they are reciprocal and less transactional
Building leadership skill requires practicing vulnerability and establishing intimacy with a trusted group
The 1-on-1 peer support model doesn't scale well, so structured small groups that meet regularly work better
Leadership is broadly defined — anyone with expertise others rely on is exercising leadership
money_quotes: There's only one thing that has reliably accelerated my development as a leader or manager, and that is forming bonds and swapping stories with my peers.
Unlike management books which speak in abstractions and generalities, stories are real and specific.
There's something sterile about management books and leadership material, something that makes it hard for me to emotionally connect my problems to the solutions they preach.
I don't want to be someone's mentor. I want to be their friend and to sometimes be helpful.
If you have expertise and people rely on you for it, this is a legit form of influence and power — in other words, that's leadership.
posts.key_insight: Leadership development is best accelerated not through formal training or hierarchical mentorship, but through reciprocal peer relationships built on vulnerability, trust, and the exchange of real, contextual stories.

posts.summary: Charity Majors distills hard-won lessons from building and running platforms at scale into ten commandments, emphasizing the need to protect the platform from individual users, center observability on user experience rather than system health, and invest in fine-grained operational controls. The advice draws on her experience at Parse and Honeycomb, focusing on what it takes to keep a multi-tenant platform alive and healthy.
key_points: Platforms must protect themselves at the expense of individual apps
Observability should center on user experience, not system-level dashboards
One-off user issues must be turned into support problems, not engineering problems
Fine-grained controls (throttles, blacklists, velvet ropes) are essential survival tools
Dogfooding your own platform is non-negotiable
money_quotes: Any time you have to think about one particular user, you have failed in some way. It doesn't scale. Just a few one-offs a day will drag you down and drown your forward momentum.
It is the job of the platform to protect itself at all costs, including at the expense of your app.

Your observability must center your users' perspective, not your own. The health of the system doesn't matter. The health of every request, and every high-cardinality grouping of requests — those are what matter.
Poorly obfuscated infrastructure decisions are usually less of a threat to your business than befuddled users are.

You will be tempted to disable them because they seem flappy and flaky and need to be fixed. But this is actually what your users are suffering through every day they use your platform. Don't disable them, fix them.
posts.key_insight: Running a platform at scale requires ruthless prioritization, user-centered observability, and the discipline to protect the platform from individual users while giving every user the tools to help themselves.

posts.summary: Engineering teams that give developers broad technical autonomy often end up with unsustainable software sprawl across languages, databases, and tools. The solution is creating a 'Golden Path' of fully-supported default components chosen by a small council of senior engineers, where developers can still choose non-standard tools but must support them entirely on their own. This preserves developer agency while creating natural pressure toward convergence and operability.
key_points: Software sprawl is common among high-performing teams that give engineers autonomy over technical decisions
A small council of senior engineers should define a Golden Path of supported default components
The Golden Path should be fully supported (upgrades, monitoring, on-call) while non-standard choices are self-supported
Teams should allocate real engineering time (e.g. 25% for three months) to consolidate onto the Golden Path
The process should be sustainable with rotating council members and regular review cycles
money_quotes: There actually seems to be a direct link between teams that give engineers lots of leeway to own their technical decisions and that team's ability to hire and retain top-tier talent, particularly senior talent.
Nobody HAS to use these components … but if they don't, they're on their own. They will have to support it themselves.
Your engineers are currently used to picking the best tool for the job by optimizing locally. What you need to do is start building your muscles for optimizing globally.
There should be friction around adding new components to your stack. Add in haste, repent at leisure, as they say.
It's not easy. I never said it was easy, I said it was simple.
posts.key_insight: The best way to reverse software sprawl without killing developer autonomy is to make the standardized path so well-supported that choosing anything else carries a real personal cost.

posts.summary: Engineering management is a career change, not a promotion, and managers face a critical fork: stay technical as a line manager (practicing the engineer/manager pendulum) or climb the organizational ladder—a mostly one-way trip that progressively erodes technical skills and narrows career options. New managers should commit to a minimum two-year tour of duty, resist the advice to completely stop coding, and understand that their technical skills begin seriously deteriorating after 3-5 years away from hands-on work.
key_points: Management is a career change requiring a minimum two-year commitment to develop new skills and reward mechanisms
Technical skills erode significantly after 3-5 years of pure management, making it increasingly hard to return to engineering
Managers should stay hands-on with non-critical-path engineering work to maintain effectiveness
Climbing the organizational ladder is mostly a one-way trip with shrinking options and increasing dependence on factors outside your control
The intersection of technical and people skills is what gives engineering managers outsized impact
money_quotes: Management is a career change, not a promotion, and nobody is very good at it when they first start.
Your technical skills stop advancing when you become a manager, and instead begin eroding.
Don't suck up all the oxygen: you'll stunt the growth of your team.
If management isn't a promotion, then returning to hands-on work isn't a demotion, either.
The mere existence of a ladder compels us all to climb.
posts.key_insight: Engineering managers must consciously resist the default ladder-climbing narrative and periodically return to hands-on technical work, because the intersection of technical and people skills is what makes them effective—and that intersection has an expiration date.

posts.summary: Traditional logging best practices were designed for monolithic systems with expensive disks and simple architectures, but they fail completely in distributed systems where investigation requires computation across many services. Engineers need to replace log lines and log levels with arbitrarily wide structured events — one per request per service — that capture rich context and feed into systems capable of ad hoc querying.
key_points: Traditional logging best practices (be stingy, only log errors, use debuggers) were correct for monolithic systems but are counterproductive in distributed architectures
In distributed systems, your only view of the system is through the instrumentation of your code — debuggers and local disk are irrelevant
The hardest debugging problem in distributed systems isn't fixing the code, it's figuring out where the problematic code is
Structured events should capture one wide blob per request per service, making the incremental cost of capturing more detail nearly zero
Engineers need to unlearn legacy logging best practices — reframing from 'logs' to 'structured events' helps break old mental models
money_quotes: Your only view of the system is through the instrumentation of your code.

The hardest part isn't usually debugging the code, it's figuring out where is the code you need to debug. Or what the errors or outliers have in common from the perspective of the code. Fixing the code itself is often comparatively trivial, once found.
The more you think of structured events as logs, the more tempted you may be to apply the old set of best practices. So just don't think of them as logs at all.
Most importantly, the incremental cost of capturing more detail about the request per service is nearly zero.
No more regexps at all, for fuck's sake.
posts.key_insight: Legacy logging best practices actively harm observability in distributed systems — engineers must replace scattered log lines with wide structured events that capture full request context, enabling the computational investigation that modern systems demand.

posts.summary: This guest post (by George Chamales, introduced by Charity Majors) provides a practical framework for engineers trying to champion third-party observability and SaaS tools within organizations that have security and compliance concerns. It acknowledges that both the champion's optimism and the security team's caution are valid, and offers a structured worksheet for evaluating risks vs. rewards when proposing a new external service.
key_points: Both engineering and security perspectives on third-party services are valid — the rewards are real, but so are the risks
Security certifications and polished security pages are not reliable indicators of actual security posture
The risk of a third-party service is highly dependent on how you use it and what data you send
Operational metrics are categorically less sensitive than PII/PHI, which changes the risk calculus
A structured worksheet approach helps put risks and rewards in context for management and security teams
money_quotes: To you, it's an opportunity to deploy new features in a flash, juice your team's productivity, and save boatloads of money. To your security and compliance teams, it's a chance to lose your customers' data, cause your applications to fall over, and do inordinate damage to your company's reputation and bottom line.
The good news is, you're absolutely right. The bad news is, so are they.
History is full of compromised companies who had very pretty security pages and certifications.

There's risk no matter what you do – bringing in the service is risky, doing nothing is risky. You can only mitigate risks up to a point. Beyond that point, it's the rewards that make risks worthwhile.
Outsourcing your observability to a vendor for whom it's their One Job is almost always the right call, in terms of money and time and focus — and yes, even security.
posts.key_insight: Successfully championing a third-party service requires acknowledging that security teams' concerns are legitimate while building a structured case that the rewards outweigh the risks — and that doing nothing carries its own risks.

posts.summary: This guest post by Lilly Ryan advises development teams on how to work effectively with internal security teams when adopting third-party observability services. It emphasizes building empathy by understanding the security mindset through exercises like the 'Land Astronaut' game, preparing for compromise, and investing in long-term cross-team relationships rather than treating security as a gate to pass through.
key_points: Security teams and development teams have fundamentally different mindsets — builders vs. flaw-finders — and bridging that gap requires deliberate empathy exercises
The 'Land Astronaut' game helps developers practice negative thinking to anticipate security objections before they arise
Preparing for compromise before approaching security leads to more successful negotiations
Embedding developers with security teams and vice versa builds rapport and prevents adversarial dynamics
Security should be included from day one of the development process, not bolted on at the end
money_quotes: The biggest problems in technology are never really about technology, but about people.

Software development tends to attract positive-minded people who love creating things and are excited about the possibilities of new technology. Software security tends to attract negative thinkers who are skilled at finding all the flaws in a system.

Even in 2019, the task of ensuring that your product and stack are secure and well-defended is often left until the end of the development cycle. This contributes a great deal to the combative atmosphere that is common.
Bringing security people into the development process much earlier builds rapport and prevents these adversarial, territorial dynamics.
posts.key_insight: Getting security buy-in for observability tools is fundamentally a people problem, not a technology problem — developers must practice empathy by adopting the security team's negative-thinking mindset before approaching them with requests.

posts.summary: This guest post by Andy Isaacson covers the operational phase of rolling out a third-party observability vendor, from launch through long-term maintenance and eventual sunset. It emphasizes designating an Integration Lead to manage vendor relationships, regularly revisiting security checklists as usage evolves, and maintaining empathy with security teams throughout the lifecycle.
key_points: Designate an explicit Integration Lead for each vendor relationship to avoid diffusion of responsibility
Revisit security checklists whenever usage patterns change or new data categories are added
Practice empathy during rollout incidents rather than assigning blame, which poisons organizational trust
Have a sunset plan for vendor relationships before you need one
Treat vendor integrations as operational systems requiring monitoring, alerting, and regular security review
money_quotes: While it's easy, wearing the champion hat, to dismiss any issues found by looking for someone to blame, ultimately this poisons trust within your organization and will hamper success.
Let the experts surprise you; folks you might not expect can step up when given a chance.
Having one person explicitly responsible for a relationship lets them pay attention to those vendor emails, updates, and avoid the tragedy of the 'but I thought you were' commons.
If they can't support the same SSO that you use everywhere else and can't add it — or worse, look confused when you mention SSO — that's probably a sign you should consider a different vendor.
posts.key_insight: Successfully operationalizing a third-party vendor integration requires designated ownership, ongoing security collaboration, and treating the vendor relationship as a living operational concern rather than a one-time procurement decision.

posts.summary: Charity Majors reflects on her relationship with pain as a career growth strategy, from brute-forcing her way past public speaking terror to her painful tenure as CEO of Honeycomb. She concludes that while leaning into acute pain can build skills, chronic pain in a role you hate will only diminish you—sometimes the only way to succeed is to redefine success.
key_points: Brute-forced through public speaking fear by giving 38 talks in two years until it became an ordinary skill
Stepped down as CEO of Honeycomb after realizing chronic misery was not a problem she could power through
Distinguishes between acute pain (useful teacher) and chronic pain (destructive hijacker of reward pathways)
Warns against treating misery as a virtue—pain narrows your vision and shuts down creativity
The wisdom to know which pain to lean into and which to walk away from is the real career skill
money_quotes: Pain is a terrific teacher, but pain is an acute response. Chronic pain will hijack your reward pathways, your perspective, your relationships, and every other productive system and leave them stunted.
Misery is not a virtue. Pain is awful. That's why it's so powerful and primal.

Leaning in to pain can be powerful if you have the agency and ability to change it, or practice it to mastery, or even just adapt your own emotional responses to it. If you don't or you can't, leaning in to pain will kill you. Having the wisdom to know the difference is everything.
Turns out not every problem can be powered through on a high pain tolerance. The collateral damage starts to rack up. Sometimes the only way to succeed is to redefine success.
Everything is hard when you're in pain. Your creative brain shuts down. Basic life functions become impossible tests.
posts.key_insight: Leaning into acute pain builds skills, but chronic pain in a misaligned role will destroy your capacity—knowing which battles to power through and which to walk away from is the defining career wisdom.

posts.summary: Friday deploy freezes are a symptom of deeper infrastructure problems, not a responsible engineering practice. Rather than accepting deploy fear as normal, teams should invest in CI/CD pipelines, observability tooling, and smaller deployments to make shipping code a non-event. Slowing down deploys actually worsens reliability by batching changes and degrading engineers' judgment.
key_points: Deploy anxiety is the single largest source of technical debt in many organizations
Research shows deploy velocity and lower error rates reinforce each other, not oppose each other
Friday freezes cause Monday debugging nightmares from batched changes
Rules and policies inhibit the development of good engineering judgment
Deploy freezes used as virtue signaling for work/life balance actually work against that goal
Every deploy should be owned by the developer who made the changes, not whoever is on-call
money_quotes: Anxiety related to deploys is the single largest source of technical debt in many, many orgs. Technical debt, lest we forget, is not the same as 'bad code'. Tech debt hurts your people.

If you are a software company, shipping code is your heartbeat. Shipping code should be as reliable and sturdy and fast and unremarkable as possible, because this is the drumbeat by which value gets delivered to your org.

Policies (and enumerated exceptions to policies, and exceptions to exceptions) are a piss-poor substitute for judgment. Rules are blunt instruments that stunt your engineers' development and critical thinking skills.
Blocking Friday deploy is not a mark of moral virtue; it is a physical bash script patching over technical rot. And technical rot is bad because it HURTS PEOPLE.
A team's maturity and efficiency can be represented by how long it takes to get their shit into users' hands after they write it.
posts.key_insight: Friday deploy freezes treat the symptom instead of the disease — teams should invest in fast, small, observable deployments rather than accepting fear of shipping as normal.

posts.summary: Charity Majors lays out 17 intrinsic reasons why engineers should think twice before becoming managers, from the loss of creative joy and technical growth to the emotional toll, fewer job opportunities, and the fact that most rewarding 'management skills' can be practiced as a senior IC. She argues for honest self-assessment before making the leap, while acknowledging management is worth trying with a full two-year commitment.
key_points: Management is overhead — managers don't directly create value, making them first to be cut in layoffs and valued negatively in acquihires
The dopamine drip from building things disappears in management, and it takes 1-2 years to rewire your brain for the slower rewards of coaching others
Most rewarding management skills (mentoring, technical leadership, running projects) can be practiced as a senior IC without the downsides
A manager's effectiveness is grounded in trust and relationships, making skills far less transferrable than engineering skills
Formal authority is a blunt, fragile instrument — any time you have to tell someone what to do using it, you have failed and your actual influence decreases
money_quotes: Any time you have to tell someone what to do using your formal authority, you have failed in some way and your actual influence and power will decrease. Formal authority is a blunt, fragile instrument.
As a baseline for acquihires, the acquiring company will estimate a value of $1 million per engineer, then deduct $500k for every other role being acquired.
That dopamine drip in your brain from fixing problems and learning things goes away, and it's … real tough.
Introspection and self-knowledge are absolutely key to being a decent manager, and lord knows we need more of those. So don't kick off your grand experiment by lying to yourself, ok?

If you are a strong tech lead and you convert to management, it is your job to begin slowly taking yourself out of the loop as tech lead and promoting others in your place. Your technical skills will stop growing at the point that you switch careers, and will slowly decay after that.
posts.key_insight: Engineering management involves trading away creative joy, technical growth, job mobility, and personal time for indirect influence through others — and most of the rewarding parts of management can be done as a senior IC without making that trade.

posts.summary: Traditional monitoring accumulated excessive paging alerts because teams relied on pattern-matching from past failures to debug monolithic systems. With distributed systems, this approach collapses because failures are infinite and unique, so teams should delete all paging alerts and replace them with SLO-based alerts that only fire when users are actually impacted, using observability tools for debugging instead of alert clusters.
key_points: Traditional monitoring built up libraries of paging alerts as a debugging crutch for pattern-matching recurring failures in monolithic systems
Distributed systems have an infinitely long list of failures that almost never recur, making per-scenario alerts wasteful
The modern approach is to page only on SLOs or end-to-end health checks that correlate to user-impacting events
Observability replaces the need for scattershot paging alerts by letting you systematically trace problems from high-level signals to root cause
If everyone is on call, management has a duty to ensure on-call doesn't suck by keeping alert noise low
money_quotes: Delete all your paging alerts, then page only on e2e alerts that mean users are in pain.

Frankly, any time you get paged about a distributed system, it should be a genuinely new failure that requires your full creative attention. You shouldn't just be checking your phone, going 'oh THAT again', and flipping through a runbook.
Instead of a limited set of recurring error conditions, you have an infinitely long list of things that almost never happen… except that one time they do.
Those paging alerts were a crutch, and now you don't need them anymore.
Every paging alert out of hours should be as serious as a heart attack, and this means allocating real engineering resources to keeping tech debt down and noise levels low.
posts.key_insight: In the era of distributed systems and observability, paging alerts should be radically reduced to only user-impacting SLO violations, because the old model of accumulating per-failure alerts was really a debugging crutch that observability tools now replace.

posts.summary: Charity Majors explains that startup hiring rejections are almost never about a candidate being 'not good enough,' but rather about constrained slots, team composition needs, and fit alignment. She walks through eleven honest reasons candidates don't get hired at Honeycomb, emphasizing that most are about organizational constraints and team dynamics rather than individual merit.
key_points: Startup hiring is defined by scarcity — few slots tied to critical company goals, not an open door for anyone who passes a bar
Teams need diversity of level, background, and skills — not just the most senior or credentialed people
Communication skills are foundational to high-performing teams and are weighted heavily in hiring
Being too senior can be just as much a reason for rejection as not being senior enough
The core hiring question isn't 'how good are they?' but 'what will they need to be successful and can we provide it?'
money_quotes: People stay at shitty jobs far, far longer than they ought to, just because interviews can be so genuinely crushing to your spirit and sense of self.
A team staffed with nothing but extremely senior developers will be a dysfunctional, bored and contentious team where no one is really growing up or being challenged as they should.
We believe that if you can explain what you did and why, you can definitely do the work, and the reverse is not necessarily true.

If we brought you in for an interview, we already think you're awesome. Period. Now we're just trying to figure out if you narrowly intersect the skill sets we are lacking that we need to succeed this year.
The phrase you would hear over and over is not 'how good are they?', but 'what will they need to be successful? can we provide the support they need?'
posts.key_insight: Startup hiring rejections are almost always about constrained organizational needs and team composition rather than a judgment of individual worth, and the real question hiring managers ask is whether they can set a candidate up for success — not whether the candidate is 'good enough.'

posts.summary: SLOs serve as an abstraction layer that protects engineering teams from micromanagement, priority hijacking, and feature pressure by establishing clear boundaries and shared ownership of service quality. When stakeholders sign off on SLOs, they agree that reliability is the top priority, giving teams leverage to push back on disruptions and justify technical debt work. The framework works bidirectionally—teams that exceed their SLOs should stop polishing and ship features.
key_points: SLOs establish a perimeter around engineering teams that prevents unauthorized interference from management and stakeholders
Getting stakeholders to sign off on SLOs makes them co-owners of production excellence, not just feature demanders
Error budgets provide a common language for evaluating risk without deeply understanding every individual situation
SLOs work bidirectionally: they justify reliability work when budgets are tight and push teams to ship when budgets are healthy
The real value of SLOs is organizational, not just technical—they formalize trust between teams
money_quotes: SLOs are a powerful weapon to wield against micromanagers, meddlers, and feature-hungry PMs.
Senior management might not understand that fifty thousand things a day are broken, and the team cannot stop and fix or care about every single one of them.
When you are exceeding your SLO you can stand to add more chaos to the system, so turn the knob back up.
SLOs are the right level of abstraction for agreements between teams within an org for the same reasons they are useful between companies.
Instead of having to deeply understand and evaluate the risk of every single situation in its own unique glory, we have a simple common language for evaluating risk in terms of error budgets.
posts.key_insight: SLOs are not primarily a technical tool for measuring uptime—they are an organizational contract that defines how other teams and managers are allowed to interact with your engineering team.

posts.summary: Charity Majors argues that blocking Friday deploys is a symptom of unhealthy deployment practices, not a solution. Drawing on her experience at Parse and Honeycomb, she demonstrates that the real path to protecting engineers' work-life balance is building a tight build/ship/observe feedback loop with observability, single-changeset deploys, and observability-driven development culture.
key_points: Blocking Friday deploys is a smell indicating your deploys are risky, and the practice makes things worse by batching changes and elongating deploy intervals
The three keys to safe deploys are observability, observability-driven development culture, and deploying one changeset at a time immediately after merge

At Parse (blocked Friday deploys, batched changes), engineers were woken up multiple times per week; at Honeycomb (continuous deploys with observability), pager volume dropped to roughly one alert per week
80-90% of deploy problems can be caught by the developer watching their own changes roll out before users or alerts ever notice
Systems are more stable when you always ship consistently and quickly; carving out no-deploy windows actually reduces stability
money_quotes: You cannot have it both ways, where you block off a bunch of undeployable time AND you have robust, resilient, swift deploys.

That tight, short feedback loop of build/ship/observe is the beating heart of a healthy, observable distributed system that can be run and maintained by human beings, without it sucking your life force or ruining your sleep schedule or will to live.
Our systems were more stable when we always shipped right after the changes were merged. Our systems were less stable when we carved out times to pause deployments.

The idea of going back to a world without observability, a world where one deployed and then stared anxiously at dashboards — it was unthinkable. It was like I was being asked to give up my five senses for production.
Congratulating ourselves for blocking Friday deploys is like congratulating ourselves for swatting ourselves in the face with the flyswatter.
posts.key_insight: Blocking Friday deploys treats the symptom while worsening the disease — the real fix is investing in observability and tight deploy feedback loops so that every deploy, any day of the week, is safe and fast.

posts.summary: Charity Majors presents a formal bill of rights and responsibilities for engineering managers, complementing an earlier post focused on engineers. The rights emphasize that managers deserve honest feedback, respect for their role, decision-making authority, emotional support, and the freedom to leave management. The responsibilities cover hiring, inclusive culture, giving feedback, planning, self-care, and staying vulnerable.
key_points: Managers deserve the same honest, timely feedback they're expected to give their reports
Management should be treated with equal respect and importance as individual contributor work
Managers have final say on hiring, firing, and leveling but should drive consensus
Self-care and vulnerability are non-negotiable responsibilities for managers, not optional luxuries
No one should be pressured into management — it must be a genuine choice
money_quotes: Cultivate an inclusive culture and redistribute opportunity. Fuck a pedigree. Resist monoculture.
You cannot take care of others unless you first practice self-care. You damn well better take vacations. Real ones.
Always say the hard things, but say them with love.
You do not have to be a manager if you do not want to. No one will ever pressure you.
posts.key_insight: Engineering managers deserve explicit rights — including honest feedback, emotional support, and the freedom to step away — alongside their heavy responsibilities, because codifying both keeps organizations honest as they scale.

posts.summary: A veteran engineer who became a manager to drive sweeping DevOps transformation now feels trapped — bored by management's daily grind but afraid that returning to IC work means losing power and relevance. Charity argues that the two-year mark is the critical window to switch back, that senior ICs who've managed are 'supremely powerful beings,' and that the real next challenge is fighting to make organizations where management isn't a promotion.
key_points: The two-year management mark is a pivotal moment — it only gets harder to return to technical work after this point
Senior ICs with management experience wield outsized influence through leadership-by-influence rather than authority
Technical decisions should be owned by technical people, not managers — but this only happens when senior ICs assert that ownership
Feeling 'small' as an IC reflects broken organizational hierarchies, not an inherent truth about the role
The tech is always the easy part — organizational and leadership skills are the real differentiator
money_quotes: You are going to be attuned to power dynamics and ways of influencing that you never were before — and remember, leadership is primarily exercised through influence, not explicit authority. Senior ICs who have been managers are supremely powerful beings, who tend to wield outsize influence.
If management is not a promotion, then going back to an engineering role should not feel like a demotion!
Organizations are created afresh each and every day — not created for us, but by us.

If the senior ICs don't assert their leadership, managers are unlikely to give it to them. If managers try, but senior ICs don't inhabit their power, eventually the managers just shrug and go back to making all the decisions.
The tech is the easy part. The tech will always be the easy part.
posts.key_insight: The engineer-manager pendulum isn't a demotion — senior ICs who've tasted management become uniquely powerful agents of organizational change, but only if they assert ownership over technical leadership rather than ceding it to the management hierarchy.

posts.summary: Charity Majors debunks common myths about observability adoption — that you only need it at scale, that it's harder than monitoring, and that it's purely technical — arguing it's valuable at any scale and actually easier than traditional monitoring. She then provides a practical playbook for engineering managers to drive observability adoption, centered on finding an internal champion, aligning incentives through on-call ownership, and knowing when to use managerial authority.
key_points: Observability is valuable at any scale, not just for large distributed systems — it compounds in value the earlier you adopt it
Observability is actually easier than traditional monitoring because it maps naturally to how developers think about code
Observability is a sociotechnical problem requiring cultural changes like on-call rotations and blameless culture, not just tooling
The most effective adoption strategy is finding an internal engineering champion rather than top-down mandates
Managers should reserve the right to overrule teams in rare cases, and being proven right earns back authority with interest
money_quotes: It is never not better to be able to see what the fuck you are doing!

Most teams are out there shipping opaque, poorly understood code blindly — shipping it out to systems which are themselves crap snowballs of opaque, poorly understood code. This is costly, dangerous, and extremely wasteful of engineering time.
As a rule of thumb, I always assume that managers can't convince engineers to do things: only other engineers can. But what you can do instead is set up an engineer to be your champion.
If you cannot wield your power when circumstances call for it, then you don't fucking have real power — you have unilaterally disarmed yourself, to the detriment of your org.
If you order something to be done, and it turns out in the end that you were right? You earn back all the power you expended on it plus interest.
posts.key_insight: Observability adoption is fundamentally a sociotechnical challenge best driven by empowering an engineering champion with context and autonomy, not by managerial decree — though leaders must be willing to use authority when the stakes demand it.

posts.summary: Charity Majors argues that observability is being deliberately watered down by monitoring vendors who rebrand their existing metrics, logs, and tracing products. True observability, borrowed from control theory, means the ability to ask any question about your system's internal state without predicting those questions in advance, which requires arbitrarily-wide structured events with high cardinality and high dimensionality — something no traditional monitoring tool provides.
key_points: Observability is a term from control theory meaning you can understand any internal system state by asking questions from the outside
Metrics are hostile to observability because pre-aggregation permanently discards the connective tissue between requests
True observability requires one arbitrarily-wide structured event per service per request containing full context
High cardinality and high dimensionality support are non-negotiable table stakes for observability tooling
Developers waste over 40% of their day because they are flying blind without proper observability
money_quotes: This is such a reliable bait and switch that any time you hear someone talking about 'metrics, logs and traces', you can be pretty damn sure there's no actual observability going on.
We are shipping code we don't understand, to systems we have never understood. Some poor sap is on call for this mess, and it's killing them.

If you instrument your code, watch it deploy, then ask 'is it doing what I expect, does anything else look weird' as a habit? You can build a system that is both understandable and well-understood. If you can see what you're doing, and catch errors swiftly, it never has to become a shitty hairball in the first place. That is a choice.
Observability as I define it is like taking off the blindfold and turning on the light before you take a swing at the pinata.
Vendors need to stop trying to confuse people into buying the same old bullshit tools by smooshing them together and slapping on a new label.
posts.key_insight: True observability is the ability to ask arbitrary questions about unknown system states without predicting them in advance, and vendors redefining it as 'metrics, logs, and traces' are actively preventing the industry from escaping its current dysfunction.

posts.summary: Charity recommends Barbara Ehrenreich's 'Blood Rites' as quarantine reading, summarizing its thesis that humanity's sacred feelings toward war originate not from innate aggression but from our evolutionary history as prey animals who venerated blood sacrifice. The book argues we are the 'nouveaux riche of predators' who go to war because we were once prey, and this discomfort drives our ritualization of violence. She connects this framework to understanding cultural phenomena from horror movies to Tiger King.
key_points: Ehrenreich's thesis reframes war not as a product of innate human aggression but as a vestigial relic of blood sacrifice rituals from when humans were prey
The book challenges traditional scholarship including feminist theories about violence being intrinsically male
Our sacred feelings toward war descend from veneration of blood sacrifice, which itself reenacted our role inversion from prey to predator
The framework explains many cultural phenomena: horror movies, cannibalism taboos, religious sacraments, and even Tiger King
money_quotes: If you kill one person, you're a murderer and we shut you out from society; kill ten and you are a monster; but if you kill thousands, or kill on behalf of the state, we give you medals and write books about you.
We are the nouveaux riche of predators, constantly re-asserting our dominance to soothe our insecurities.
We go to war not because we are predators, in other words, but because we are prey — and this makes us very uncomfortable!
The primal fear of being food still resonates through our culture in so many sublimated ways.
posts.key_insight: Ehrenreich's 'Blood Rites' argues that humanity sacralizes war not because we are natural predators but because we are former prey animals, and our ritualization of violence is a vestigial expression of ancient blood sacrifice born from that deep evolutionary insecurity.

posts.summary: Charity Majors shares the advice Honeycomb gave employees during the pandemic: acknowledge that what's being asked is literally impossible, shed everything except your top priority, and spend zero energy on performing normalcy. The core message is that 30 minutes of focused creative energy on the biggest problem beats five hours of distracted availability theater.
key_points: What everyone is being asked to do during the pandemic is literally impossible — and that's okay to acknowledge
Success comes not from doing everything but from saying no to the right things
Performative busyness wastes precious energy while doing nothing to advance real goals
Optimize for focused creative time on the biggest problem, not for appearing available
Having bad days is expected — teams exist to carry each other through the hardest spots
money_quotes: We succeed as a company not by doing everything on our list, but by saying no to the right things; by NOT-doing enough most things so we can focus on the few things we have identified that matter most.

I would rather have 30 minutes of your creative energy applied to our biggest problem today than five hours of your distracted split-brain, juggling, trying to keep up with chat and seem like you're as available per usual today.

Please spend zero energy on performing like you're doing work, or acting extra-responsive, or keeping up a front like things are normal and you're doing fine. That performance costs you precious energy, while doing nothing to get us closer to our goals.
If you're not a parent, remember that you too are allowed to feel overwhelmed and underwater. Just because some have it even harder, doesn't invalidate what you're going through.
posts.key_insight: In crisis periods, leaders should explicitly give teams permission to shed work and stop performing normalcy, redirecting all available energy toward the single highest-impact problem.

posts.summary: Charity Majors describes Honeycomb's experiment of discussing hypothetical ethical dilemmas monthly as a team. She argues that ethical problems are fractally hard and that practicing these discussions when stakes are low helps teams make better decisions under pressure, builds trust, and amplifies employee voices.
key_points: Ethical problems appear simple on the surface but reveal fractal complexity the closer you examine them
Honeycomb holds monthly company-wide discussions of hypothetical ethical scenarios
Practicing difficult decisions in low-stakes settings prepares teams for real crises
Transparency and employee voice-amplification build trust for when fast decisions are needed
Companies aren't democracies but leaders have a responsibility to understand where their team stands
money_quotes: Ethical problems are hard. Even the ones that seem straightforward on the face of them get stickier the closer you look at them. There are more stakeholders, more caveats, more cautionary tales, more unintended consequences than you can generally see at face value. It's like fractal hardness, and anyone who thinks it's easy is fooling themselves.

People make the worst possible decisions when they're taken off guard, when they are in an unfamiliar situation (and often panicking). Talking through a bunch of nightmare scenarios is a way for us to exercise these decision-making muscles while the stakes are low.

We all get to experience what it's like to hear a problem, have a kneejerk reaction .. then peeling back the onion to reveal layer after layer of dismaying complexities that muddy our snap certainties.
We believe that companies are created every day by the people who show up to labor together, so those people have a right to know most things.
posts.key_insight: Regularly practicing ethical decision-making as a team when stakes are low builds the trust and judgment needed to navigate real crises well.

posts.summary: Charity Majors argues that individual engineering productivity cannot be meaningfully measured through metrics, as creative professional work is too complex to reduce to discrete measurements. She advocates instead for outcome-based management, team-level health metrics, well-calibrated managers with technical judgment, and DORA metrics as proxies for team effectiveness.
key_points: Individual productivity metrics for engineers are gameable and counterproductive
The team is the irreducible unit of ownership, not the individual engineer
Managers must be technical to cultivate independent judgment about contributions
DORA metrics track team-level engineering productivity effectively
Outcome-based management requires trusting engineers with autonomy
