money_quotes: To the extent you can reduce a job to a set of metrics, that job can be automated away.
The team is the irreducible unit of ownership.

It is condescending as fuck for leadership to treat engineers like task-crunching interchangeable cogs. It reveals a deep misunderstanding of how sociotechnical systems are developed and sustained (plus authoritarian tendencies, and usually a big dollop of personal insecurity).
Some of the hardest and most impactful engineering work will be all but invisible on any set of individual metrics.
It is in those gaps where we feel trusted to act that we find joy and autonomy in our labor, where we do our best work as skilled artisans.
posts.key_insight: Engineering productivity should be evaluated at the team level through outcomes and DORA metrics, not through individual metrics that incentivize gaming and ignore the most impactful work.

posts.summary: Charity Majors shares her hard-won process for making boba (tapioca pearls) at home after months of trial and error during quarantine. The post is a practical cooking guide born from her ADHD-driven food fixation on boba, covering sourcing safe ingredients, avoiding ruined pans, and achieving the perfect chewy texture.
key_points: ADHD can manifest as intense food fixations lasting months to years
Many cheap boba brands from Amazon made her ill - sourcing matters
The key to not ruining pans is using a massive amount of water relative to boba
Boba requires a long cook (20-25 min boiling + 20-25 min resting) and doesn't keep well
money_quotes: Last year I was diagnosed with ADHD, which was a great surprise to me (if no one else).
One year it was manhattans (makers mark, sweet vermouth and bitters) and I seriously worried I was becoming an alcoholic.

I finally figured out how to make my own boba. This was NONTRIVIAL. It took the sacrifice of countless pans and far too many nights doubled over with nausea and stomach cramps, and months of trial and error.
The tapioca dust turns to gummy that sticks to the sides and bottom and gets baked on like a motherfucker.
posts.key_insight: This is a personal lifestyle post about making boba at home, revealing Charity's characteristic voice and humor outside her usual tech topics, driven by her ADHD tendency to fixate intensely on one thing at a time.

posts.summary: Charity Majors explains how to identify your team's critical path by asking 'what makes you money?' — the essential business operations that justify waking someone up at 3am. She argues teams should keep this list compact and explicit, which also clarifies that everything else can wait until morning.
key_points: Your critical path is defined by what makes you money and what your customers care most about
The critical path should be as compact and well-defined as possible
Being explicit about critical path means being explicit that everything else can wait
This is fundamentally a question about service level objectives (SLOs)
Resist the urge to page on every service deterioration — not everything is worth waking someone up for
money_quotes: Like, if you could only deploy three end-to-end checks that would perform entire operations on your site and ensure they work at all times, what would they be?

The idea here is to draw up a list of the things that are absolutely worth waking someone up to fix immediately, night or day, rain or shine. That list should be as compact and well-defined as possible.
This allows you to be explicit about the fact that anything else can wait til morning, or some other less-demanding service level agreement.

Someone will usually seize this opportunity to argue that absolutely any deterioration in service is worth paging someone immediately to fix it, day or night. They are wrong, but it's good to flush these assumptions out and have this argument kindly out in the open.
posts.key_insight: Your critical path is defined by asking 'what makes us money?' — keep the list of page-worthy operations as small as possible so you can explicitly acknowledge that everything else can wait.

posts.summary: Charity Majors argues that most engineers become managers to compensate for organizational dysfunction rather than the selfless reasons they claim in interviews. She contends that in healthy organizations, engineers shouldn't need to become managers for career progression, pay equity, or decision-making access, and that the only legitimate reason to try management is simple curiosity.
key_points: Most engineers become managers primarily due to organizational dysfunction, using institutional powers to fix what makes them miserable
In healthy orgs, you shouldn't have to become a manager for career progression, pay equity, or access to decision-making
The socially acceptable reasons people give for becoming managers rarely tell the full story
Management should be demystified and treated as a service role, not a promotion or dominance position
Anyone curious about management should get a shot at it, because the swiftest cure for romanticizing it is actually doing it
money_quotes: In my experience, most engineers become managers primarily due to organizational dysfunction. When you become a manager you acquire certain institutional powers, and you can use those powers to change the thing that makes you miserable.
You should not have to become a manager to have career progression, pay equity, access to information, to be included in the decision-making process, even to set company strategy.
The swiftest cure for this delusion is … actually becoming a manager.

Management is a role where you are granted certain institutional powers, at the expense of other powers, freedoms and benefits. Many people who try management figure out pretty quickly that it's not for them. Formal powers are, in many ways, the weakest powers of them all.
posts.key_insight: Most engineers become managers to hack around organizational dysfunction, but in a healthy org the only honest reason to try management is simple curiosity — and the fastest way to cure the obsession with it is to actually do it.

posts.summary: Charity Majors tears apart the concept of centralized 'command center' war rooms for monitoring applications, arguing they are a waste of money rooted in outdated thinking. She identifies two fatal flaws: graphs can be automated and only catch known problems, and the people watching graphs aren't the ones who can actually fix issues. The approach actively harms engineering feedback loops by insulating developers from the consequences of their code.
key_points: Centralized monitoring command centers are an outdated, wasteful approach to operations
Monitoring dashboards only catch known unknowns while most real problems are unknown unknowns
Only the engineers who build and maintain systems can effectively diagnose and fix them
Adding a human monitoring layer insulates developers from feedback about their code's behavior
Bugs are exponentially harder to fix the longer they go undetected
money_quotes: That extra human layer is worse than useless; it is actively harmful. By insulating developers from the consequences of their actions, you are concealing from them the information they need to understand the consequences of their actions.
The only people who can swiftly diagnose and fix complex systems issues are the people who build and maintain those systems, and those people are busy building and maintaining, not watching graphs.

The best time to find a bug is as soon as possible after writing it, while it's all fresh in your head. If you let it fester for days, weeks, or months, it will be exponentially more challenging to find and solve.

This path leads into a death spiral where alarms are going off constantly (yet somehow never actually catching the real problems), people getting burned out, and anyone competent will either a) leave or b) refuse to be on call.
posts.key_insight: Centralized war rooms for monitoring are actively harmful because they break the essential feedback loop between developers and the production behavior of their code, and no amount of dashboard-watching can replace the contextual knowledge of the engineers who built the systems.

posts.summary: Charity Majors argues that the perception of management as a promotion is the root cause of bad managers persisting in tech, because returning to engineering is seen as a demotion that nobody wants to accept. She advocates for treating management as a lateral career change and support role, not a hierarchical step up, and provides a concrete roadmap for companies to restructure power dynamics so that engineering and management are seen as equally valuable but different paths.
key_points: Management is culturally perceived as a promotion, making the return to engineering feel like a demotion that most people resist
Bad managers persist because they can't swallow the ego and pay hit of stepping back to IC work
The engineer-manager pendulum produces better leaders than the one-way management ladder
Most engineers become managers to cope with organizational dysfunction, not because they genuinely want to manage people
Companies should structurally equalize pay, power, and prestige between IC and management tracks
money_quotes: Any technical company should work hard to center and celebrate the work being done to build the product and make customers happy. Management is overhead, to be brutally frank about it, and we should not design organizations that would lead any rational, ambitious person to aspire to be overhead, should we?
Formal authority is the weakest form of power, and you should resort to using it rarely. Good leaders lead by influence and persuasion, weak leaders with 'because I said so.'
The ladder is a trap. There are an order of magnitude fewer jobs for each rung you ascend. Meanwhile, the higher you climb the farther removed you are from the work most find meaningful.
Managers tend to accrue power over time, like a gravitational force. In order to counterbalance this drift, managers need to consciously push power out to others.
Where someone sits on the org chart should not determine that person's ability to drive change, nor should their preference for tech problems or people problems.
posts.key_insight: The cultural perception of management as a promotion traps bad managers in roles they shouldn't hold and starves organizations of great technical leaders who fear the stigma of "stepping down" — and companies must actively restructure power, pay, and prestige to break this cycle.

posts.summary: Engineers frustrated about slow career progression often overlook that promotion opportunities are situational — not every company has the right openings, oxygen, or need at every level at every time. Title inflation from making exceptions undermines leveling systems, and engineers should strategically evaluate whether their current environment actually offers the growth opportunities they seek rather than passively waiting to be promoted.
key_points: Promotion opportunities are finite and situational — companies can only support so many senior+ engineers with meaningful high-level work
Title inflation from making exceptions destroys the integrity of leveling systems
Generalists level up faster than specialists
Engineers stuck at the senior bubble often want someone to tell them what to do, which is the opposite of what senior engineers do
It's much easier to level up at fast-growing companies where there's more work than workers
money_quotes: Not every opportunity exists at every company at every time.
Literally no company can possibly make use of a top-heavy eng org stuffed with senior+ engineers, if all of them are expected to demonstrate company-wide impact or global impact every review period.

Many engineers get stuck on the bubble getting to senior because they are impatient and want a map. They just want someone to tell them what to do. Which is the very opposite of what a senior engineer does.
The only leverage you have when people try to get you to make exceptions is if you can honestly say, 'no exceptions.' Give in just once, and your moral authority evaporates.
There is sooo much title inflation in this industry already. People are given the title 'senior engineer' in just 3-5 years, need I say more??
posts.key_insight: Career progression depends less on individual merit and more on whether your current environment has the structural oxygen — the right ratio of work, people, and need — to support your advancement.

posts.summary: On-call responsibility is a two-way compact: engineers should own their code in production, but management must ensure on-call doesn't suck by investing in tooling, tracking alerts, protecting on-call time from project work, and treating every out-of-hours page as urgent. Managers who fail to hold up their end of this bargain deserve to lose their engineers.
key_points: On-call is a handshake between engineering and management — engineers own their code, managers ensure the experience is sustainable
Engineers on call should be freed from normal project work to focus on fixes, tooling, and guardrails
Every out-of-hours alert should be treated seriously and the underlying cause fixed, even if it slows product development
Reliability work and technical debt deserve equal standing with product work on the roadmap
It's possible to make on-call fully opt-in, a source of pride, and something engineers genuinely look forward to
money_quotes: It is engineering's responsibility to be on call and own their code. It is management's responsibility to make sure that on call does not suck. This is a handshake, it goes both ways, and if you do not hold up your end they should quit and leave you.
Tossing it off to ops after tests pass is nothing but a thinly veiled form of engineering classism, and you can't build high-performing systems by breaking up your feedback loops this way.
Night time pages are heart attacks, not diabetes.
Any asshole can write some code; owning and tending complex systems for the long run is the hard part.
Your greatest enemy is how easily you accept the status quo, and then make up excuses for why it is necessarily this way.
posts.key_insight: On-call is a mutual compact where management owes engineers sustainable conditions — protected time, good tooling, alert tracking, and treating every page as urgent — and failing this obligation justifies engineers walking out the door.

posts.summary: Charity Majors advises an engineer who became the most senior person on their team by default (at their first job) to leave and start over elsewhere. She argues that true seniority comes not from deep familiarity with one system, but from having learned and leveled up across multiple teams, stacks, and organizations over 5-8 years.
key_points: Being the most experienced person on a single team doesn't make you a senior engineer — it just means you're familiar with that system
True seniority requires 2-3 different teams, stacks, and roles over 5-8 years
Starting over as a beginner is humbling and clarifying, and builds the skill of learning itself
Experiencing how different organizations solve problems teaches you to reason about tradeoffs rather than clinging to one blessed path
Building teams where beginners aren't miserable requires leaders who remember what it's like to start fresh
money_quotes: There is a world of distance between being expert in *this system* and being an actual expert in your chosen craft. The second is seniority; the first is merely .. familiarity
Think of every job like an escalator — a 50-foot high escalator that takes about two years to ride to the top. But once you've summited, you stall out.
Nobody wants to work with seniors who clutch their authority with a white knuckled grip. We want to work with those who wear it lightly, who remember what it was like in our shoes.
It is *good* for us to start over with beginner eyes. It's humbling, it's clarifying, it's a cleanse for the soul.
You will have proven to yourself that your success wasn't just a one-off, that you can be dropped into any situation, learn the local ropes and succeed. You will be a senior engineer.
posts.key_insight: True engineering seniority isn't earned by becoming the expert on one system — it's forged by starting over multiple times across different teams and stacks until you can succeed anywhere.

posts.summary: Charity Majors argues that CI/CD lead time—from code merge to production deploy—should be under 15 minutes, and that achieving this is central to building high-performing engineering teams. She provides a comprehensive, community-sourced catalog of anti-patterns and bottlenecks that slow down build pipelines, organized by category from test design to AWS infrastructure choices.
key_points: CI/CD lead time from merge to deploy should be under 15 minutes
Long lead times make high-performing teams nearly impossible
Instrument your build pipeline with spans and traces to identify bottlenecks
Only run tests affected by your change, not the full suite
Any reduction in lead time pays compounding benefits forever
money_quotes: It's nigh impossible to have a high-performing team with a long lead time, and becomes drastically easier with a dramatically shorter lead time.

If you get CI/CD right, a lot of other critical functions, behaviors, and intuitions are aligned to be comfortably successful and correct with minimal effort. If you get it wrong, you will spend countless cycles chasing pathologies.
I suggest pouring your time and creative energy into figuring out how closely you can approximate the ideal given what you have, instead of compiling all the reasons why you can't achieve it.

Most of the people who tell me they can't do this are quite wrong, turns out. And even if you can't down to 15 minutes, ANY reduction in lead time will pay out massive, compounding, benefits to your team and adjacent teams forever and ever.
posts.key_insight: A 15-minute CI/CD pipeline isn't a luxury—it's the foundation that aligns everything else for engineering team success, and most teams who think they can't achieve it are wrong.

posts.summary: The post addresses the common struggle managers face in feeling worthwhile when they're no longer the ones building things. Charity argues that managerial satisfaction is fundamentally different from engineering satisfaction — it's slower, less visible, and lacks the dopamine drip of shipping code. She offers practical frameworks for locating your impact in big-picture outcomes, small human moments, and crisis situations, while calling out the performative selflessness managers are expected to display.
key_points: New managers should expect to feel lost for at least two years and not worry about self-assessment during that period
Manager satisfaction operates on fundamentally different timelines and feedback loops than engineering work
The expectation that managers must derive all fulfillment from others' success is performative and dishonest
Managerial value accrues over time through trust-building, not through any single visible action
In a crisis, the real test is whether you're a source of calm or a situation to be managed
money_quotes: As an engineer, you get that very tactile sense of merging code, solving puzzles and incrementally pushing the business forward. It has a rhythm and a powerful drip, drip, drip of dopamine, and as a manager you will never ever feel that.
There is nothing wrong with wanting credit for your work, and affirmation and validation, and there is nothing inherently noble about not wanting those things.
Nobody wants a needy manager.

Do your feelings become other people's problems? This reflects your ability to regulate your own feelings and emotional impulses under stress, and most of us quite overestimate our own power to self-regulate.
In the end, your worth as a manager is seen in the trail you leave behind.
posts.key_insight: Managerial worth is measured not in daily visible output but in the long trail of trust, team health, and crisis resilience you build over years — and pretending you don't need recognition for that work is dishonest.

posts.summary: Charity Majors argues that engineering managers must have solid technical foundations, not just management skills, because modern engineering work involves complex sociotechnical systems where social and technical problems are inseparable. While non-technical managers can occasionally succeed through either exceptional personal qualities or organizational structures that compensate for their gaps, these are exceptions that shouldn't define hiring strategy.
key_points: Engineering management requires at least 5-7 years of engineering experience at a comfortable senior level, but superior engineering skills don't translate to better management
Non-technical managers can work in two scenarios: rare unicorn individuals with exceptional empathy, or organizational structures that pair them with strong tech leads
The manager-plus-tech-lead split creates fragile dynamics where the TL holds disproportionate power and accountability lines blur
Modern engineering problems are sociotechnical - purely social or purely technical problems rarely exist in isolation
Managers should stay technical by writing code outside the critical path, but their primary job is being interruptible and available
money_quotes: You can't recruit a Starbucks manager to manage a software engineering team any more than you would recruit an NFL coach to coach your Olympic gymnastics team. If you want to coach a team, you gotta know the game.

These days we all work on, and from within, these incredibly complex sociotechnical systems and feedback loops that are so complex, there is rarely any problem that is purely 'social', 'tools', or 'technology'. It is all entwined and interconnected.

You need to be able to look at a team that isn't shipping very fast and figure out whether it's because of under-skilled engineers, legacy code, a weak product culture, a leaky CI/CD pipeline, or a team that has given up and lost confidence in itself.
Managers need to stop writing code in the critical path. They cannot allow themselves to become a bottleneck, so they shouldn't write key features or contribute to the really interesting parts.

If you try to separate the social from the technical, and assign different domain owners to each, you are slicing up the ownership and responsibility lengthwise. You're setting yourself up for squabbles about whose fault it really was.
posts.key_insight: Engineering managers need both technical and social skills because modern software teams operate within complex sociotechnical systems where separating those domains creates fragile organizational structures and muddied accountability.

posts.summary: Charity Majors argues that early-career engineers should prioritize experience and career growth over salary optimization. She pushes back against the common recruiter advice to never go backward on compensation, framing it as short-sighted thinking that treats salary as a one-way escalator rather than a tool for building long-term career value.
key_points: Engineers should be willing to take pay cuts to escape the 'premature senior' trap and invest in career growth
The first decade of a career is too early to optimize for salary over experience
Compensation should be based on labor value to the company, not past salary
Retaining financial optionality through savings and avoiding lifestyle inflation enables better career decisions
Recruiter advice to 'never go backward on compensation' can be actively harmful
money_quotes: Your career is the single most valuable asset you have — it's a multimillion dollar appreciating asset, and you should curate and guide it with an eye toward longevity.
Consider it an investment in your long term career. Think of the extra money your company is paying you now as a hostage premium on top of your real market value.
Salary is not a one-way escalator that you hop on after school, and gracefully exit at the peak upon retiring.
We have the unfathomable luxury of being incredibly well compensated for what we do. What is that luxury worth if we don't use it to liberate ourselves, to facilitate happiness and fulfillment?
This is a strong argument for trying to live well within your means, save religiously, and always have fuck you money in the bank.
posts.key_insight: Early-career engineers should treat compensation as a tool for career optionality rather than a number that must always go up, because optimizing for salary over experience in your first decade can trap you in roles that stunt long-term growth.

posts.summary: Teams that fail to implement true continuous deployment — where every merge auto-deploys to production in under 15 minutes — pay an enormous hidden cost in engineering headcount and organizational complexity. The delay between writing code and shipping it creates compounding pathologies that force teams to grow far larger than necessary to accomplish the same work.
key_points: True CI/CD means every merge triggers an automatic deploy to production in 15 minutes or less with no human intervention
Deploy delays create exponential headcount costs: a 6-person team with day-long deploys needs 24 people to do the same work
Slow deploys create compounding pathologies: longer diffs, manual processes, batched deploys, blame-hunting
Organizations respond to deploy friction by adding SRE teams, build engineers, managers, and PMs — all of which could be avoided
money_quotes: Most painful of all are the teams who have done all the hard parts — wired up continuous integration, achieved test coverage, etc — but still deploy by hand, thus depriving themselves of the payoff for their hard work.
That's how much your neglect of internal tools and kneejerk fear of autodeploy might be costing you.

The more delay you add into the process of building and shipping code, the more pathologies multiply, and you find yourselves needing to spend more and more time addressing those pathologies instead of making forward progress for the business.
You would be moving more swiftly and confidently as a small, killer team than you ever could at your lumbering size.
posts.key_insight: Deploy delays don't just slow you down linearly — they create compounding organizational pathologies that force exponential growth in headcount and complexity to accomplish the same work a small team with continuous deployment could handle.

posts.summary: The post provides a comprehensive guide of interview questions candidates should ask to evaluate whether a potential manager and management culture will be healthy or toxic. Charity argues that candidates should treat interviews as a two-way evaluation, offering specific questions for both managers and engineers that reveal red flags like involuntary managers, lack of career development paths, and evasive communication styles.
key_points: Ask the same questions of multiple people and compare answers, giving extra weight to underrepresented minorities' experiences
The most revealing interview question is how work gets decided and allocated — evasiveness or pure top-down assignment are red flags
Test whether a manager can tell you 'no' directly during the interview as a trust signal
Management should be a lateral move, not a promotion, with viable paths back to engineering
Ask for specific situations rather than hypotheticals — any half-decent manager can BS on hypotheticals
money_quotes: You are all highly compensated subject matter experts who can find jobs relatively easily; there's no reason all the judgment and critique should flow in a single direction.

Most of all, can you get the manager to tell you 'no' on a thing you clearly want during the interview? Anyone who will look you straight in the eye and say 'no, and here's why', is someone you can probably trust to be straight with you down the line, in good times and bad.
Trust me, you never want to work for someone who was pushed into management against their will and still seems openly bitter about it.
A manager's One Job is to make sure that shit doesn't happen. It really is worth trying to find a good one you can trust.
Ask about specific situations. Any half-decent manager can BS on hypothetical stuff.
posts.key_insight: Interviews are a two-way street, and candidates can reliably detect bad management culture by asking pointed questions about how work gets allocated, whether managers can say no directly, and whether management is treated as a peer role rather than a promotion.

posts.summary: Charity Majors argues that no amount of extracurricular contributions — interviewing, mentoring, speaking, community building — can compensate for failing to deliver on your core job responsibilities. She illustrates through a story how high-visibility side work actually becomes evidence against you when your primary deliverables are slipping. The post urges engineers to ruthlessly prioritize their 'One Job' first, and to honestly reassess whether they're in the right role if they chronically can't find motivation for it.
key_points: Your core job responsibilities always take priority over extracurricular contributions
Extras become strikes against you when your primary work is slipping
Feeling overwhelmed can make all commitments feel equal weight, but they aren't
Chronic inability to motivate on core work may signal you're in the wrong role
Core responsibilities should never add up to 40+ hours per week
money_quotes: When someone isn't meeting the basic expectations for their core responsibilities, it doesn't matter how many other wonderful things they are doing. In fact, those things can become strikes *against* them.

When you are meeting expectations for your One Job — and you don't necessarily have to be dazzling, just competent and predictable — then picking up other work is a sign of initiative and investment. But when you aren't, you get no credit.

If you did only your core responsibilities and none of the extras, your job should not be in any danger. But if you don't do the core parts, no matter how many extras you do, eventually your job probably will be in danger.
In the long run, you'll be happier and more successful if you are truly engaged by the work you were hired to do, not just by the extracurriculars.
posts.key_insight: Extracurricular contributions only count as signs of initiative when you're already meeting expectations on the core work you were hired to do — otherwise they become evidence of poor prioritization.

posts.summary: Charity Majors responds to the backlash from her previous post about prioritizing your core job responsibilities, addressing objections about glue work, DEI labor, gendered dynamics, and management failures. She clarifies that while extracurricular work like ERGs and mentoring should factor into promotions, they cannot substitute for fulfilling the technical requirements you were hired for. She acknowledges her advice assumes a high-trust environment and encourages engineers to take ownership of career clarity even when managers fail them.
key_points: Your core job responsibilities come first, but extracurricular work like ERGs and glue work should absolutely factor into promotions when core duties are met
Promotion requires fulfilling the technical requirements of the next level — DEI work and glue work alone cannot substitute for that
Engineers should proactively seek clarity on their core responsibilities rather than waiting for good management
The advice assumes a high-trust work environment, which not everyone has the privilege of operating in
Job descriptions are living documents that should be revisited and negotiated, not assumed to be static
money_quotes: If you ARE competently performing your core responsibilities as a database engineer, and are performing them at the next level, then all your additional work for the company — on DEI, ERGs, mentoring, interviewing, etc — adds up to a MASSIVELY compelling body of work, and a powerful argument for promotion.
I am writing this post because good managers are in scarce supply, and I don't want your career to be on hold until you happen to get a good one.
You shouldn't HAVE to do their job for them. But why let a shitty manager hurt your career any more than you must?
Communication and persuasion are what upper levels are all about.
A successful career in sociotechnical systems means leveling up your social repertoire as well as your technical skills.
posts.key_insight: Fulfilling your core job responsibilities is the foundation that makes all your other valuable contributions — glue work, DEI labor, mentoring — count toward career advancement rather than being seen as distractions from unfinished work.

posts.summary: Charity Majors advises someone preparing for an exit interview to focus not on self-protection or bridge-preservation, but on being effective. She provides tactical advice for structuring exit feedback as a written, evidence-based case that ties toxic behavior to material consequences and delivers it to people with actual power to act on it.
key_points: Exit interview advice to 'not burn bridges' is self-serving HR advice that perpetuates harm
The goal of exit feedback should be effectiveness, not self-protection
Structure feedback like an essay: high-level thesis supported by specific incidents and material consequences
Always put exit feedback in writing — written words are harder to ignore or distort
Most exit feedback gets filed away and ignored; bypass HR inertia by delivering directly to people with power
money_quotes: That's exactly the kind of quivering, self-serving, ass covering advice you'd get from HR. It's exactly the kind of advice that good people use to perpetuate harm.
This is your last chance to do the people you care about a solid, and you don't want to waste it.

The sad fact is that most exit feedback is dutifully entered in by a low ranking employee who makes a third your salary and has no reason whatsoever to rock the boat, after which it gets tossed in a folder or the trash and is never seen again.
HR doesn't care about your feedback … but they care if they think their boss saw it and cares about it.
What's the point of having power if you won't extend yourself now and then on behalf of those who don't?
posts.key_insight: Exit interviews should be treated not as a ritual to survive unscathed but as a strategic opportunity to drive change, structured with written evidence and delivered to people who actually have power to act.

posts.summary: When hiring an engineering manager, companies should always give internal candidates first consideration before looking externally, since effective management requires deep knowledge of the tech stack, existing relationships, and team trust. The post argues that management should be treated as a lateral career change rather than a promotion, and that the best engineer is rarely the right choice for manager.
key_points: Internal candidates should always get first crack at management openings due to their existing relationships and technical context
Management is a career change, not a promotion — decouple it from leadership and status
Don't promote your best engineer to manager; there's no positive correlation between engineering skill and management skill
Engineers need at least 5-7 years of hands-on experience before considering management
The team's opinion of a proposed manager matters more than leadership's opinion
money_quotes: There is no correlation that I've observed between someone's engineering skills and management skills – no positive correlation, at any rate.
You won't be doing them any favors by asking them to switch careers earlier in their tenure, and you might well be inflicting permanent damage on their career.
You should never inflict a manager on a team who doesn't want to receive them in that role, no matter how qualified they seem on paper.
Hiring isn't about finding the 'best person', it's about crafting the strongest team.
Being a 'really good person' is not enough to do the job; 'good people' inflict immense harm every day without meaning to.
posts.key_insight: Engineering management should be treated as a lateral career change that prioritizes internal candidates with strong relationships and team trust, not as a promotion for your best technical performer.

posts.summary: Charity Majors lays out a practical roadmap for engineering managers to develop their careers, arguing that growth should focus on craft mastery rather than climbing the org chart. She covers concrete skills from confrontation and public speaking to understanding power dynamics and optimizing sociotechnical systems, emphasizing that most managers were dropped into the role with little guidance.
key_points: Career growth for managers should focus on craft mastery, not title chasing
Understanding power dynamics is a prerequisite for managing people responsibly
Building a trusted peer group of other managers is the single most important thing you can do
Confrontation avoidance is the fastest way to poison relationships and lose trust
Managers should build transferable skills, not just org-specific institutional knowledge
Periodic swings back to engineering keep managers employable and sharp
money_quotes: It's one thing to be unaware of power dynamics and structural inequities as an individual contributor, and another when you wield formal hiring and firing powers on behalf of the org. You can really mess people up that way, and it doesn't matter if the harm came from your ignorance instead of malice.
Such a huge part of being a manager is making sure you don't turn your problems into other people's problems, or make your feelings other people's problems. And that's hard.

If you hate confrontation, I'm sorry honey, but you have to push through that barrier ASAP. It is the one thing that will hold you back, poison your relationships, result in loss of trust, and ultimately hurt your direct reports faster than anything else.

Building a long and rewarding career in tech is not about chasing titles or levels, it's about learning to follow your curiosity (and learning to love the uncertain feeling of being out of your depth.)
Managers have a tendency to slip into a pattern of only doing things or growing in ways that are useful to the org, but useless to them personally. Don't do this! Be more selfish.
posts.key_insight: Engineering management career development should focus on mastering the craft itself — peer networks, confrontation skills, self-awareness, and sociotechnical thinking — rather than chasing promotions up the org chart.

posts.summary: Charity Majors maps out seven common career inflection points for engineering managers, arguing against rigid career planning and hierarchical ladder-climbing in favor of self-knowledge, preserving optionality, and following intrinsic motivation. She emphasizes that management is a career change (not a promotion), advocates for the engineer-manager pendulum, and urges people to prioritize doing work they genuinely love over chasing titles and status.
key_points: Career planning is overrated — focus on self-knowledge and preserving optionality instead of optimizing for hierarchy
Management is a career change, not a promotion, requiring a full reset to beginner mindset for at least two years
Junior engineers pushed into management too early lose technical credibility and optionality, with women disproportionately affected
There is a chronic shortage of good engineering managers — once you have the skill set, you'll be tapped repeatedly whether you want it or not
Senior leaders who miss engineering should have the courage to go back — the prestige of titles is mostly in your head
money_quotes: Management isn't a promotion, it's a change of career. And if you aren't up for resetting the dial to 'NOOB' for a couple years, with all of the ups and downs, anxieties and discombobulations, then you aren't up for management.
When you're an engineer, it seems like managers hold all the power. Two or three years into a managerial role, you should be thoroughly cured of that illusion.
It takes more effort not to be a manager once you're capable of it.

Nobody wants to report to a manager who doesn't want to be there, or is doing the job begrudgingly. Your complaining is toxic, and makes it impossible for you to coach others to have a healthy relationship with their roles.
The higher you climb the ladder, the further removed you are from the work that brings most people intrinsic joy, that feels real.
posts.key_insight: Instead of planning careers around hierarchical advancement, engineers and managers should cultivate self-knowledge, preserve optionality by rotating between roles, and have the courage to choose work they genuinely love over titles and status.

posts.summary: Static dashboards are poor debugging tools because they encourage pattern-matching against historical answers rather than methodical investigation of current problems. While useful as high-level system health overviews, dashboards strip away the connective tissue between events, making it impossible to correlate attributes or follow a trail of breadcrumbs to diagnose novel issues. The industry needs to move toward queryable, exploratory interfaces that let engineers interrogate their systems rather than scan pre-built graphs.
key_points: Static dashboards answer forgotten questions and encourage pattern-matching instead of systematic debugging
Metrics-based dashboards strip away event context, making it impossible to confirm correlations between seemingly related incidents
Effective debugging is iterative question-asking, not scanning graphs for visual patterns
Dashboards are most useful as a small, curated set of starter queries rather than a graveyard of past outages
The industry needs queryable, exploratory interfaces that support unknown-unknown discovery
money_quotes: That's not debugging, that's pattern-matching. That's … eyeball racing.
With metrics, you tend to find what you're looking for.

Every dashboard is an answer to a question someone asked at some point. Your monitoring system is probably littered with dashboards, thousands and thousands of them, most of whose questions have been long forgotten and many of whose source data streams have long since gone silent.
If all you have is a hammer, you CAN use it to drive screws into the wall, but that doesn't mean it's the best tool.
The overuse of them just encourages sloppy thinking, and static ones make it impossible for you to follow the plot of an outage, or validate your hypotheses.
posts.key_insight: Dashboards seduce engineers into pattern-matching against past incidents rather than methodically investigating present ones, and the industry needs to replace this habit with queryable, exploratory tools that support real debugging of unknown-unknowns.

posts.summary: Charity Majors argues that organizations should spend 20-30% of their infrastructure costs on observability, pushing back against the common instinct to minimize tooling spend. She makes the case that 'saving money' on observability by using free/open-source tools often costs far more in hidden engineering time, recruitment, and degraded developer experience.
key_points: Observability spend should be 20-30% of infrastructure costs as a rule of thumb
Engineers' time is not free — DIY observability tools have massive hidden costs in maintenance, training, and opportunity cost
Vendors bear responsibility for transparent pricing and should not optimize revenue at the expense of user experience
Good engineering orgs use good tools because they know engineering cycles are their scarcest resource
The quality of an org's tooling choices is a reliable signal of whether it hires and retains good engineers
money_quotes: You should probably spend 20-30% of infra costs on observability.

We aren't used to considering the price of our own time. We act like it's 'free' to just take an hour and spin something up … we don't count the cost of maintenance, context switching, and opportunity costs of not using the time to build something of business value.
If you want to know if an org hires and retains good engineers, you could do worse than to ask the question: 'What tools do you use, and why?'
Good orgs use good tools. They know engineering cycles are their scarcest and most valuable resource, and they want to train maximum firepower on their core business problems.
Any pricing scheme that doesn't align with best practices and users' interests will be a drag on reputation and growth.
posts.key_insight: The true cost of observability is never just the vendor bill — organizations that cheap out on tooling pay far more in hidden engineering time, attrition, and degraded capability than they ever save.

posts.summary: Charity Majors argues that software engineering interviews should include operations questions, just as ops/SRE interviews now include coding questions. She contends that operational excellence is everyone's responsibility, and that interviewing for ops knowledge both identifies engineers who will own their code in production and sets cultural expectations from day one.
key_points: The industry pushes ops engineers to learn coding but doesn't equally push software engineers to learn systems and operations skills
Software engineers must be responsible for their code after deployment, not just before
Including ops questions in SWE interviews sets expectations about ownership culture from the start
The best ops interview questions are broad and open-ended, exploring experience and depth rather than pass-fail knowledge
Engineers who view ops work as lesser create toxic dynamics and poor operational outcomes
money_quotes: Our industry has gotten very good at pressing operations engineers to get better at writing code, writing tests, and software engineering in general these past few years. Which is great! But we have not been nearly so good at pushing software engineers to level up their systems skills.

You need to balance out your software engineers with engineers who don't react to every problem by writing more code. You need engineers who write code begrudgingly, as a last resort. You'll find these priceless gems in ops and SRE.

By making ops questions part of the interview, you're establishing from the start that you run an org where operations is valued, where ownership is non-optional. This is not an ivory tower where software engineers can merrily git push and go home for the day and let other people handle the fallout.
It can be toxic when you have an engineer who thinks all ops work is toil and operations engineering is lesser-than. It tends to result in operations work being done very poorly.
posts.key_insight: Including operations questions in software engineering interviews isn't just about evaluating technical breadth — it's a cultural signal that sets expectations about code ownership and operational responsibility from the very first interaction with a candidate.

posts.summary: Charity Majors catalogs the many cognitive biases that lead engineering teams to adopt overly cautious deployment practices like 'no Friday deploys,' arguing that every common justification for slowing down deployments is materially false. She frames resistance to continuous deployment not as rational risk management but as emotional reactions retroactively dressed up in noble-sounding language.
key_points: Common deployment restrictions stem from cognitive biases, not rational risk assessment
Teams retroactively justify gut-feeling fears with the language of noble motives like 'protecting weekends'
Slowing down deploys actually increases risk — speed and small diffs are safer
The 'no Friday deploys' norm appeals to so many biases it will never fully disappear
Most teams claiming unique reasons for slow deploys are falling prey to false-uniqueness bias
money_quotes: We leap to conclusions with the wetware we have doing the best it can based on heuristics that feel objectively true, but are ultimately just emotional reactions based on past lived experience. And then we retroactively enshrine those goofy gut feelings with the language of noble motive and moral values.
Slow is dangerous for software; speed is safety. The more frequently you ship code, the smaller the diffs you ship, the less dangerous each one actually becomes.
Deploy myths are never going away for good; they appeal to too many of our cognitive biases.
Every single argument of the items in the list above is materially false.
posts.key_insight: Resistance to frequent, automated deployments is driven not by legitimate technical concerns but by a catalog of cognitive biases that make inaction feel safer than action, when in reality speed and small changes are what make deploys safe.

posts.summary: Charity Majors argues that the phrase 'breaking down silos' is a meaningless cliché that substitutes for actual critical thinking about organizational communication problems. She contends that real solutions to collaboration problems are complex and specific, and people who use vague buzzphrases almost never have the hands-on experience of actually solving these problems.
key_points: The phrase 'breaking down silos' tells you nothing about what someone actually did or what problems they solved
More communication isn't automatically better — too much can be as harmful as too little
What matters is the quality and structure of communication: who talks to whom, when, and why
Business clichés like 'breaking down silos' and 'fix your culture' are substitutes for critical thinking
People who have actually done the hard work of improving organizations talk about the specific work, not buzzphrases
money_quotes: You can't just blindly throw 'more communication' at your teams. Too much communication can be just as much of a problem and a burden as too little.
When someone says they are 'breaking down silos', whether in an interview, a panel, or casual conversation, it tells me jack shit about what they actually did.
Cliches are a substitute for critical thinking.

People who have done the hard labor of building better organizations with healthy communication flows, less inefficiency, and alignment around a single mission — people who have gotten all the people rowing in the same direction — tend to talk about the work. People who haven't, say they were 'breaking down silos.'
posts.key_insight: Organizational buzzphrases like 'breaking down silos' are red flags that signal someone lacks the specific, hard-won experience of actually diagnosing and solving complex communication and collaboration problems.

posts.summary: Charity Majors argues that 'engineering-driven' culture often becomes 'engineering-supremacy,' where technical functions are valued above all others to the detriment of the business. She advocates for equal respect across all functions — engineering, product, design, sales, marketing — because business success is what ultimately enables a company to pursue its mission and values.
key_points: Engineering-driven companies fall into two traps: either alienating business functions or believing engineering skill transfers to all domains
Honeycomb had a zero-tolerance hiring policy against engineers who disrespected sales and marketing
Business success is the prerequisite for all other aspirations — without profitability, your destiny gets taken out of your hands
Cross-functional curiosity and respect can be fostered through deliberate cultural practices like deal write-ups, demo days, and recognition channels
Top-tier business talent is attracted to companies where they know they'll be respected as equals
money_quotes: If you can't make it profitable, your destiny will inevitably be taken out of your hands and given to someone else.
We DO NOT hire engineers who talk shit about sales and marketing.
So many companies who pride themselves on being 'engineering-driven' are actually what I would call more 'engineering-supremacist'.
Every function of the business is equally challenging, creative, and worthy of respect. None of us are here to peacock; our skill sets serve the primary business goals.
Supremacy destroys balance. Always.
posts.key_insight: True engineering excellence requires treating every business function as an equal partner, because without business success, even the best engineering culture becomes a cautionary tale.

posts.summary: Charity Majors provides a comprehensive guide for job candidates to evaluate whether a company is healthy or toxic during the interview process. She argues that candidates hold more power than they realize and should rigorously scrutinize potential employers through backchannel research, probing questions about diversity, business health, team dynamics, and engineering practices. The post emphasizes that interviews are a two-way street and encourages people to leave quickly if a new job feels wrong.
key_points: Use backchannel networks and recently-departed employees to get unvarnished truth about a company
Evaluate diversity not just in headcount but in leadership, accountability, and concrete improvement plans
Companies comfortable with visible failure and genuine transparency are self-correcting and healthier
Deploy interval is a strong proxy for engineering happiness and productivity
The questions a company asks you in interviews reveal what they actually filter for, not their advertised values
money_quotes: Your labor is valuable — it is vital — and you should be scrutinizing them every bit as closely as they you.
Being self-aware and comfortable with visible failure are two of the most important self-correcting mechanisms a company can cultivate.

EVERYBODY thinks they value transparency, so I wouldn't even bother asking. Instead, ask for specific examples of leadership being forthcoming with bad news to the team, and team members delivering hard feedback or bad news to upper leadership.

The questions they ask you while interviewing you are the questions they ask everyone else. So…did they ask you about your views on diversity and team dynamics while interviewing you? Or is that not part of their filters, only their advertised persona?
You do not owe them a year of your life. Trust your instincts.
posts.key_insight: Job candidates should flip the interview power dynamic by rigorously evaluating companies through backchannels, probing questions about failure and diversity, and concrete signals like deploy frequency — and should leave without guilt if what they find doesn't match.

posts.summary: The post addresses two common anxieties people face when swinging between engineering and management roles: the fear of never getting another management opportunity, and the fear of being too rusty to return to engineering. Charity argues the first fear is misplaced since good managers are always in demand, while the second is more valid since technical skills genuinely erode over time. She advises engineers to be strategic about refreshing technical skills for both effectiveness and job security.
key_points: The fear of never getting another management opportunity is misplaced — once you've managed, the skills are visible in everything you do
Technical skills erode over time in a way management skills don't, making the return to engineering a legitimate concern
Never accept a management role until you're solidly senior as an engineer (at least 5-7 years)
After 5+ years in management, returning to engineering gets progressively harder but is still possible
Maintaining technical fluency provides job security since senior IC roles vastly outnumber management openings
money_quotes: You are more likely to spend the rest of your career fending off management "opportunities" with a baseball bat than you are to wither away, pining for another shot.
Senior engineers with management experience are worth their weight in gold. They are valuable contributors and influential teammates.

Never, ever accept a managerial role until you are already solidly senior as an engineer. To me this means at least seven years or more writing and shipping code; definitely, absolutely no less than five.

As a senior software engineer, there are fifteen bajillion job openings for you. Everyone wants to hire you. You can get a new job in a matter of days, no matter how picky you want to be about location, flexibility, technologies, product types, whatever. You've reached Peak Hire.
posts.key_insight: The engineer-manager pendulum's two anxieties are asymmetric: good managers will always be recruited back into management, but technical skills genuinely decay, making it essential to strategically refresh them for both effectiveness and career optionality.

posts.summary: Charity Majors walks back her inflammatory anti-metrics rhetoric, clarifying that metrics aren't worthless—they're just the wrong tool for observability. She draws a clear line between 'your code' (which demands observability with rich, high-cardinality events) and 'other people's code' (third-party infrastructure where metrics and monitoring are the right fit).
key_points: Metrics are mature, cheap, and aggregate well—ideal for monitoring system health in aggregate
Observability handles unknown-unknowns; metrics handle known-unknowns
Your own code demands observability with rich instrumentation; third-party code only needs metrics
Events can be decomposed into metrics, but not the reverse
Teams with observability naturally use metrics and dashboards less over time
money_quotes: You're never going to make a metrics tool like Prometheus or Datadog into an observability tool. You're just not. Observability is about unknown-unknowns, while metrics are a tool for known-unknowns.
Your code is your crown jewels, the code you need to survive and succeed as a business. It changes constantly—many times per week, if not per day.
With third-party stuff, you don't care about the code, you care about the health of the service. In aggregate.

People who have observability typically find themselves using metrics and dashboards less and less. They're simply not as versatile or useful as events that you can slice and dice and manipulate in infinite ways.
posts.key_insight: Metrics aren't bad—they're just scoped to aggregate system health and third-party infrastructure, while your own code demands the richer, event-level understanding that only observability provides.

posts.summary: The post argues that engineering managers aspiring to become directors should understand that the director role is fundamentally different from management—not just a senior version of it. Directors run the business, manage managers rather than ICs, and must excel at managing up, out, and across the organization. Getting promoted internally is preferable to being hired into a director role at a new company, and candidates should proactively signal their career goals and demonstrate org-level impact.
key_points: A director is not a super-senior manager—it's a fundamentally different role focused on running the business and knitting technology with business objectives
Internal promotion is generally better than being hired into a director role at a new company, because context and relationships matter enormously
Managing up and out (horizontally with peers) becomes essential at the director level, not optional
Directors must help managers lead using their own unique strengths rather than imposing their personal leadership style
Aspiring directors should demonstrate org-level impact by fixing systemic problems, not just excelling within their team
money_quotes: We want people in senior leadership who want to be there and are honing their skills in anticipation of an opportunity. Not 'oops, I accidentally a VP.'
Blaming something on 'politics' is usually a way of accidentally confessing that you don't actually understand the constraints someone is operating under.

The distance between managing engineers and managing managers is nearly as vast as the gulf between being an engineer and being a manager. But it's sneakier, because you don't feel out of your depth as much as you did when you became a manager.
If one person gives you a piece of hard feedback, assume 10x as many will never tell you. Be grateful. The more power you are perceived to have, the less feedback you will ever hear.
Directors run the business. They are accountable for results. You can't be bopping in and writing or reviewing code, or tossing off technical opinions. That's not your job anymore.
posts.key_insight: The leap from engineering manager to director requires a fundamental identity shift—from leading a high-performing team to running the business—and most managers underestimate how different the job really is.

posts.summary: On-call pain is not an inevitable cost of owning code in production — it's a sociotechnical problem requiring both technical fixes (alert discipline, SLOs, proper tooling) and organizational solutions (management accountability, fair rotations, humane policies). Engineering owns their code, but management owns the responsibility to make on-call sustainable, and failure to do so is a five-alarm fire that should be fixed with urgency.
key_points: On-call rotations are sociotechnical systems requiring both people and technical solutions
Engineering owns code in production; management owns making that sustainable
Alert discipline requires only two lanes: 'wake me up' and 'deal with this later'
The ideal on-call rotation has 7-8 people for sustainability
On-call problems often mask deeper organizational dysfunction
money_quotes: It is engineering's job to own their code in production. It is management's job to make sure it doesn't suck.

If management isn't holding up their end, if they don't allocate enough time to fix the underlying problems – if they run a feature factory that never stops to refactor or invest in reliability work – then on-call will never get better, and you should leave.
I would never ask a new parent to be on call. Being woken up by ONE instrument of chaos is all anyone should ever have to cope with at any given time.
Pay them so goddamn well the finance team starts squawking about the need to pay down that reliability debt.

Engineers should never have to quietly suffer the pain of flaky software and unhappy users alone. Give management pain too until they take their jobs seriously enough to see that reliability issues get fixed.
posts.key_insight: On-call suffering is never just a technical problem — it's a management accountability problem, and the two-way pact between engineering ownership and management support must be enforced for on-call to be sustainable.

posts.summary: A former CTO asking about returning to engineering should come back as a regular software engineer, not a staff+ role, and spend at least 6 months doing hands-on daily coding with a great team. The transition requires not just refreshing technical skills but re-acclimating to the rhythms of deep focus work, reconnecting with curiosity, and rebuilding empathy for production code — similar to how moving into management required learning an entirely different way of working.
key_points: Return to engineering as a regular software engineer writing code daily, not as a staff+ engineer
Tactical coding skills degrade like language fluency — you use it or lose it — while management skills persist
Start with something new rather than picking up where you left off to avoid painful stumbling through once-effortless motions
Allow at least 6 months to re-acclimate to the deep focus rhythms of engineering work
Reconnecting with the emotional intensity of shipping code and being personally responsible for production is essential
money_quotes: Writing code is like speaking a language: you use it or lose it. And just like with a language, the best way to freshen up is full immersion.

I don't think it's being overly dramatic to say that you have to allow enough time to become a different version of yourself. You can't just change personas and entire ways of being like you change your clothing.

If you try to pick up where you left off as an expert, it's really going to suck as you stumble through the motions that used to feel effortless and automatic. But if you start with something new, the friction of learning will feel ordinary and predictable instead.
Engineers respect people who respect what they do.

To me, 'software architect' rings of 'someone who can no longer write code or perform as a software engineer, who has probably been at the same company for so long that their skills and knowledge now consist entirely of minutiae about that particular company's technology.'
posts.key_insight: The best path back from CTO to engineer is full immersion in daily hands-on coding with a great team, starting fresh with new technologies rather than trying to resume where you left off, because rebuilding tactical skills and reconnecting with the emotional reality of shipping code matters more than preserving your title.

posts.summary: The post argues that people giving feedback should be mindful of the ratio of critique to affirmation they deliver, because recipients often don't know how valued they are and only remember the criticisms. Charity offers specific techniques for making positive feedback land—being specific, using the SBI framework for praise, grounding critique in overall reaction, and giving feedback that helps people grow.
key_points: Most people you value don't realize how much you appreciate them, but are acutely aware of how they fall short
Specific praise lands and sticks far better than generic compliments
Grounding critique in your overall positive reaction prevents people from hearing only negativity
Even poorly-delivered critiques can be absorbed in the context of a good relationship
Engineers tend to show interest by picking ideas apart, which can feel like pure criticism without explicit affirmation
money_quotes: I can pretty much guarantee that none of the people you love working with realize just how much you value them, but they are acutely aware of all the ways they fall short or fail you.

Even poorly-delivered critiques can be dealt with in the context of a good relationship, when the person knows how much you value them, and even the most delicately delivered criticism can be hard to hear from someone when all you ever seem to hear from them is how much you suck.
Engineers can be the worst at this, because we tend to show our interest by eagerly engaging with an idea or piece of work … by picking it apart, and chattering about all the ways it could be better.
And there's no reason to save all the nice words and praise and gratitude for someone's funeral (or when they leave the company).
posts.key_insight: Before delivering critical feedback, consider whether the ratio of critique to affirmation you've given someone accurately represents how much you actually value them—because they almost certainly don't know.

posts.summary: Charity Majors argues that modern distributed systems require a fundamental shift from traditional monitoring and metrics to observability built on arbitrarily wide, structured raw events. She explains that as monoliths gave way to microservices, old debugging tools like printf and gdb became insufficient, and engineers must adopt structured events that capture rich context per request per service hop. The post lays out the technical requirements for true observability: structured, wide, raw, sampled events organized by request ID with trace awareness.
key_points: Observability is fundamentally different from monitoring: monitoring handles known-unknowns while observability addresses unknown-unknowns
Structured events should be arbitrarily wide (200-500 dimensions) and emit one event per request per service hop
Aggregation destroys your ability to ask new questions — raw events must be stored to enable true observability
Adding dimensions to a structured event is effectively free (still one write), unlike metrics where each new metric adds linear write amplification
Client-side dynamic sampling controls costs by keeping interesting signals while discarding common useless events like health checks
money_quotes: Observability is about the unknown-unknowns. Which requires a fundamentally different mindset and toolchain.
The hard part of distributed systems is rarely debugging the code, it's figuring out where the code you need to debug is.
The difference between strings and structured data is basically the difference between grep and all of computer science.
Aggregation is a one-way trip. You can always, always derive your pretty metrics and dashboards and aggregates from structured events, and you can never go in reverse.
Structure your goddamn data. You're a computer scientist, you've got no business using text search to plow through terabytes of text.
posts.key_insight: True observability requires storing arbitrarily wide, structured raw events per request per service hop — not metrics, not unstructured logs, not pre-aggregated data — because only raw events let you ask questions you never anticipated.

posts.summary: Charity Majors reflects on the power of team rituals to create belonging, drawing from a book on workplace rituals and her own extensive experience at Linden Lab and Parse/Facebook. She argues that the best rituals emerge bottoms-up from teams rather than top-down from executives, and that they disproportionately contribute to the most meaningful workplace memories.
key_points: Rituals create a powerful sense of belonging by linking physical actions with psychological and emotional meaning
The best engineering team rituals emerge bottoms-up from peers and line managers, not from executives
Rituals normalize failure and spread knowledge organically, as with the Shrek Ears tradition for first production incidents
Celebrating painful work like tech debt cleanup reinforces that unglamorous contributions matter
Great rituals often make no sense to outsiders, and that exclusivity is part of what creates belonging
money_quotes: That ineffable sense of belonging can make all the difference between a job that you do and a role that feeds your soul.
Congratulations on breaking production — you're really one of us now!
A ritual that comes from your VP can run the risk of feeling like forced fun, in a way it doesn't if it's coming from your peer or even your manager.
Great rituals often make no sense to anybody who isn't in the know — that's part of the magic of belonging.
Coincidentally (or not), I have no memories of participating in any rituals at the jobs I didn't like, only the jobs I loved.
posts.key_insight: Engineering team rituals that emerge organically from the team itself create a powerful sense of belonging that transforms a job into a meaningful experience, and the best ones normalize failure, celebrate unglamorous work, and make no sense to outsiders.

posts.summary: Charity Majors argues that organizational hierarchies become toxic when conflated with social dominance and status, pushing talented people like her friend Molly into management roles they don't actually want. She advocates for treating hierarchy as a neutral organizational data structure rather than a measure of human worth, and calls for radical transparency, distributed decision-making power, and career paths that let people follow intrinsic motivation rather than climbing ladders by default.
key_points: Hierarchy is useful as an organizational data structure but becomes harmful when entangled with social dominance and status
Many people climb into management not because they want to manage, but because it's the only way to get decision-making power and be 'in the room'
Command-and-control leadership is poison for creative industries like software engineering
Career fulfillment comes from intrinsic motivation and following curiosity, not from externally-validated ladder climbing
Companies must practice radical transparency and actively distribute power to technical contributors to prevent hierarchy from becoming toxic
money_quotes: The "Big Lie" of hierarchy is that your organizational structure is a vertical tree from the CEO on down, where higher up is always better.
Command-and-control leadership is poison. It stifles innovation, it saps initiative, it siphons away creativity and motivation and caring.

Most people who go in to management don't do it out of a burning desire to write performance reviews. They do it because they are fed the fuck up with being out of the loop, or not having a say in decisions over their own work.
Feeling comfortable is the system blinking orange, and it should make you uneasy.
You can't trust someone's 'yes' until you hear their 'no'.
posts.key_insight: Organizations should treat hierarchy as a neutral tool for coordination rather than a status ladder, and must actively distribute power and transparency to technical contributors so people choose roles based on curiosity and fulfillment rather than chasing status.

posts.summary: Charity Majors argues that the operations profession is bifurcating: infrastructure expertise is moving to cloud providers while a new discipline called platform engineering is emerging to help software engineers self-serve and own their code in production. Platform engineering teams function as internal product teams that bridge third-party services and developer needs, representing the natural evolution of DevOps in a cloud-native world.
key_points: Operations is splitting into deep infrastructure (at IaaS companies) and broad operability (platform engineering at product companies)
Platform engineering teams operate like product teams with PMs, UX researchers, and a focus on developer experience rather than infrastructure management
The key principle of platform engineering: make it easy to do the right things and hard to do the wrong things
Strong platform teams need a mix of operations experience and software development skills, not just software engineers building developer tools
DevOps is becoming irrelevant as companies go cloud-native and no longer need to manage multiple generations of infrastructure
money_quotes: Developer cycles are the scarcest resource in your company, and you want to spend as many of those as possible on your core product: the crown jewel, the code that makes you a business.
One of the key principles of any developer platform is that it should be easy to do the right things, and hard to do the wrong things.
A strong platform team has an equally deep grounding in operations experience and software development.
Disrespecting operational expertise is the shortest path to never again sleeping through the night.
It is also on its way to becoming irrelevant. We are swiftly entering a post-DevOps world.
posts.key_insight: Platform engineering is the natural successor to DevOps, shifting the focus from managing infrastructure to enabling developers to self-serve and own their code in production through curated, product-like internal platforms.

posts.summary: Charity Majors argues that engineering leaders should be evaluated not by the size of their organizations, but by the ratio of impact to team size. She challenges the empire-building instinct in management, proposing that delivering outsized results with small, lean teams is a far better measure of leadership skill than simply accumulating headcount.
key_points: Management empire-building measures status by headcount, but team size should be a denominator, not a numerator
Coordination costs from large organizations don't benefit users and often lead to product sprawl
Constraints fuel creativity — having 'enough' engineers is usually counterproductive
Leaders should be rewarded for lean, impactful teams rather than large organizations
Scope is typically the enemy — decomposing problems beats expanding teams
money_quotes: Every achievement has a denominator.

Bigness itself is costly. There's the cost of the engineers, managers, product and designers etc, of course. But the bigger it gets, the more coordination costs are incurred, which are the worst costs of all because they do not accrue to any user benefit — and often lead to lack of focus and product surface sprawl.

Constraints fuel creativity. Having 'enough' engineers for a project is usually a terrible idea; you want to be constrained, you want to have to make hard decisions about where to spend your time and where to invest development cycles.
What if comp didn't automatically bloat with the size of your org, but rather the impact of your work divided by the number of contributors — rewarding leaders for leaner teams, not larger ones?
posts.key_insight: The true measure of engineering leadership isn't how many people you manage, but how much impact you deliver per person — every achievement should be evaluated relative to the resources consumed to produce it.

posts.summary: Charity Majors describes how Honeycomb organized their first all-hands offsite in three years, detailing their layered COVID safety approach including testing, CO2 monitors, outdoor spaces, and social distancing options. She argues that in-person gatherings are not optional luxuries for distributed companies but critical necessities, and shares a detailed retrospective on what worked and what they'd improve.
key_points: In-person offsites are a critical ingredient for distributed teams, not a nice-to-have
Layered COVID safety approach: testing, CO2 monitors, outdoor spaces, mask availability, color-coded badge stickers
Mental health and social overload were treated as seriously as physical safety concerns
86% of the company attended and 100% of attendees said it was valuable
Key improvements identified: longer nap breaks, COVID safety marshals, trust-but-verify testing, and not making rules you can't keep
money_quotes: Getting together in human meatspace is expensive, it's annoying, it's awkward and uncomfortable and inconvenient … and it is necessary. It's not optional or a "nice-to-have," it is a critical ingredient of the recipe that makes high-performing distributed teams work.
Take care of yourselves. Do what you need to do to be fully present while you're here, rather than here 100% of the time.
Our error here was not in failing to stick to the 650 ppm rule, but in making a rule we couldn't keep.
There is no possible way we could have made the kind of connections, learned the same lessons, and formed the kind of bonds we did if we had tried to do this as a remote event.
posts.key_insight: Distributed companies must treat in-person gatherings as essential infrastructure, not perks, and can do so responsibly by taking a layered approach to safety and being honest about what worked and what didn't.

posts.summary: Charity Majors argues that organizations should decouple deploys (building, testing, and rolling out code) from releases (changing user experience), because using the deploy process to control what users see is slow, flaky, and unreliable. Feature flags solve this by letting teams deploy code continuously while controlling user-visible changes independently, enabling progressive delivery and safer testing in production.
key_points: Deploys and releases are fundamentally different actions that most organizations wrongly conflate
Feature flags decouple deploys from releases, turning user experience changes from engineering tasks into product/marketing tasks
Feature flags enable progressive delivery, beta testing, and safe testing in production
Combined with observability tools, feature flags become a superpower for comparing behavior across flag states
You don't need a heavyweight SaaS solution to start—even a simple config database table works
