money_quotes: Deploying software is a terrible, horrible, no good, very bad way to go about the process of changing user-facing code.

Deploys are an engineering task, but releases can be done by product managers—even marketing teams. Instead of trying to calculate when to begin deploying by working backwards from 10:00 a.m., you simply flip the switch at 10:00 a.m.
Feature flags are a scalpel, where deploys are a chainsaw. Both complement each other, and both have their place.
Everything gets faster, which instills more confidence, which means everything gets faster. It's an accelerating feedback loop at the heart of your sociotechnical system.
Fixing a single character mistake takes the same amount of time as doubling the feature set!
posts.key_insight: Decoupling deploys from releases via feature flags transforms shipping from a high-stakes engineering event into a routine, low-risk operation that non-engineers can control.

posts.summary: Charity Majors argues that the 'architect' role is often a locus of organizational dysfunction, where brilliant engineers are turned into glorified project managers who lose their technical skills over time. She contends that architecture should be a core engineering skill embedded in teams, not a separate role that unbundles decision-making authority from responsibility for results. The post explores several healthier patterns including embedded architects, architecture working groups, and engineer-architect pendulum swings.
key_points: The architect role tends to attract anti-patterns: unbundling decision-making from responsibility, and design becoming untethered from execution
Making architecture 'someone else's problem' produces weaker engineers and worse systems, similar to how outsourcing testing to QA departments did
Technical decisions should be made by the people who have to live with the consequences and carry the pager
Architects who stop coding become trapped: unable to get comparable jobs elsewhere, they often become the single biggest obstacle to progress
Better patterns include embedded staff+ engineers, architecture working groups of practicing engineers, and pendulum swings between architect and engineer roles
money_quotes: Only the people responsible for building software systems get to make decisions about how those systems get built.
When you make architecture 'someone else's problem' and scrap the expectation that it is a core skill, you get weaker engineers and worse systems.
Specialists are not here to do the job for you, they're to help you do the job better.
Some of the most useless people I've ever met were engineers who were 'promoted' to architect many, many years ago, and have barely touched an editor or production shell since.

A lot of companies are using some of their best, most brilliant senior engineers as glorified project manager/politicians to paper over a huge amount of organizational dysfunction, while bribing them with money and prestige.
posts.key_insight: Architecture is a core engineering skill that should be embedded in teams, not externalized into a separate role that severs the feedback loop between decision-making and living with the consequences.

posts.summary: Charity Majors reflects on her journey from being an open-source zealot who dismissed software vendors to becoming one herself, and the karmic experience of having her expertise discounted because she's now 'selling something.' She offers practical advice for vendor-side engineers to maintain credibility by leading with bias disclosure, engaging competitors' strongest arguments, and being generous with expertise.
key_points: Open source culture breeds an often unexamined contempt for software vendors that doesn't hold up under scrutiny
Most production-caliber code is written by people who were paid to write it, not volunteers
Good software has tremendous value, and the people who build it deserve to recoup a slice of that value
Sales is about repeated games and relationships, not one-time tricks
Vendors can maintain credibility by disclosing bias, engaging competitors' best arguments, and being their own biggest critic
money_quotes: I built something because I cared.
The overwhelming majority of production-caliber code is written by somebody who was paid to write it — not by volunteers.

Good software has tremendous value. It unlocks value and creates value, it pays enormous ongoing dividends in dollars and productivity, and the people who build it, support it, and bring it to market fully deserve to recoup a slice of the value they created for others.
It's a shitty, self-limiting stance to take as a default. We are all here to build things, not tear things down.
Never do a deal unless you'd do both sides of the deal.
posts.key_insight: The open-source world's reflexive contempt for software vendors is a self-limiting bias that ignores how much value paid software creates, and vendor-side engineers can maintain credibility by leading with transparency about their biases rather than hiding them.

posts.summary: Charity Majors argues that companies should 'choose boring technology culture' by focusing on organizational health rather than cultural innovation. She distinguishes between formal organizational culture (which should be stable and load-bearing) and informal culture (which emerges naturally from people when the formal structure is healthy). The core thesis is that business success earns you innovation tokens, and most companies should spend those tokens sparingly on culture, instead focusing on executing well.
key_points: Culture should serve the business, not exist for its own sake — business success earns innovation tokens to spend on cultural experiments
Distinguish between formal organizational culture (structural, load-bearing) and informal culture (emergent, bottoms-up, fun)
Organizational health — minimal politics, minimal confusion, high morale, high productivity — trumps cultural innovation
Leaders should build stable formal culture that creates safety for informal culture to thrive naturally
Success and achievement are the real fuel for workplace joy and bonding, not manufactured fun
money_quotes: If culture means everything, then culture means nothing.

You want formal culture to be boring, stable, reliable, load-bearing…because this creates a safe structure for people to bring the humor, the fun, the joy, the delight, without any fear of building collapse.
An organization that is healthy will inevitably get smarter over time.
You don't gather at work for the fun of it, you come together to do great things.
People are fucking weirdos. If you create an emotionally safe zone and the conditions for success, informal culture will thrive.
posts.key_insight: Great workplace culture isn't something leaders manufacture — it emerges naturally when they focus on organizational health and business success, creating the stable foundation that frees people to bring their own joy and creativity.

posts.summary: Charity Majors recounts how Linden Lab's tradition of wearing Shrek ears after breaking production created a psychologically safe culture around failure long before 'blameless retrospectives' existed as a concept. The opt-in ritual transformed what could be shame into celebration, teaching engineers that failure is inevitable, constant, and even funny. While acknowledging the practice might not translate to modern workplaces, she argues it was a brilliant piece of social engineering that made transparency about mistakes infectious across the entire company.
key_points: Linden Lab's Shrek ears tradition predated and functionally served as 'blameless retrospectives' before the industry developed that language
The ritual was opt-in and spread organically beyond engineering to sales, marketing, and accounting through voluntary adoption
Failure was reframed as inevitable and even celebrated, especially in a pre-DevOps era running massively distributed systems without modern tooling
The practice taught three core lessons: it is safe to fail, failure is constant, and failure is hilarious
Modern sensibilities rightly make such practices harder to implement today, which Majors sees as a sign of progress in workplace culture
money_quotes: The Shrek ears were a brilliant piece of social jujitsu.
Failure is both inevitable and ridiculous, and no one is going to get mad at you or fire you when it happens.
If you never break production, you probably aren't doing anything that really matters or taking enough risks.
The Shrek ears taught us that you fail, you laugh, you drink whiskey, you move on.
Making something goofy is the best way to make it stick.
posts.key_insight: Workplace rituals that transform failure from shameful to celebratory are powerful tools for building psychologically safe engineering cultures, but they must evolve as our understanding of workplace dynamics matures.

posts.summary: Charity Majors addresses how engineers at different career stages can drive change on their teams without formal authority. She argues that influence comes from good judgment, strategic relationship-building, and picking the right battles, offering tactical advice for junior engineers, new tech leads, and managers on how to introduce changes effectively.
key_points: Junior engineers should focus on learning fundamentals rather than trying to change established systems
New tech leads should understand existing systems before proposing changes and start with developer experience wins
Controversial ideas should be socialized one-on-one before presenting to a group to avoid triggering defensive reactions
Managers should aim to have others champion their ideas rather than pushing changes themselves
When overwhelmed by what to fix first, start with either observability or shortening the deploy pipeline
money_quotes: The actual best way to persuade people to listen to you is to make good decisions and display good judgment. Each of us has an implicit reputation score, which formal power can only overcome to an extent.
This is an apprenticeship industry. You're like a med student right now, who is just starting to do rounds under the supervision of an attending physician.

You are always going to get people's absolute worst reactions when you spring something on them in a group setting; any anxiety or dismay will be amplified tenfold. By letting them reflect and ask questions in private, you're giving time for their better selves to emerge.

Instead of having a shitty dynamic where engineers are attached to the old way of doing things and you are 'dragging them' into the newer ways against their will, you are inviting them to partner with you.
Put your glasses on before you start driving down the freeway, please.
posts.key_insight: Driving change without authority requires building credibility through good judgment, socializing ideas one-on-one before group settings, and picking battles that deliver visible wins for the team.

posts.summary: New managers often over-optimize for being liked by their direct reports while neglecting the broader organizational responsibilities of the role. Helicopter management—coddling people, preventing failure, and over-identifying with your team—can harm both the organization and the individuals you're trying to help. Great management requires balancing care for your team with strategic direction, stakeholder management, and serving the organizational system fairly.
key_points: Management is far more than coaching direct reports—it includes planning, stakeholder management, managing up, and contributing to the broader organization
Helicopter management prevents people from learning hard lessons and developing autonomy, ultimately harming them
A manager beloved by their team but ineffective organizationally is a net negative for the company
Engineers often fail to apply systems thinking to organizational decisions, defaulting to cynicism about leadership
Caring deeply is essential but insufficient—it must be balanced with strategic effectiveness and organizational responsibility
money_quotes: The system can't survive if every manager is gaming the rules on behalf of their team. The system only works if every manager is playing fair.
If you haul someone over the finish line, that is not a success. If you're going to be having the same hard conversations with them again in one month, three months, six months…that is not success.

All that my coddling ultimately did was teach them the wrong lessons, and prevent them from learning the right ones. It delayed those lessons by a few years, and made learning them all the more painful when they finally came.
It's entirely possible for a manager to be beloved by their entire team, while having a corrosive effect on the system around them.
You can be friends with your team, just like parents can be friends with their children, but the friendship doesn't come first. Your formal role comes first.
posts.key_insight: The most common rookie manager mistake is over-optimizing for your team's happiness and loyalty at the expense of organizational effectiveness, and the hardest lesson is that truly caring for people sometimes means letting them struggle.

posts.summary: When trust is low in a working relationship, every communication gets interpreted in the worst possible light, creating a death spiral. The post offers concrete tactical advice for rebuilding trust through overcommunication, tentative language, engineered positive interactions, and explicitly stating intent — drawn from the author's experience rebuilding her fractured cofounder relationship.
key_points: Low trust causes people to interpret neutral statements as hostile, creating a death spiral where every interaction deepens the rift
Trust gets rebuilt through small, positive interactions — not grand gestures
Speaking tentatively and overcommunicating intent prevents misinterpretation in low-trust environments
The 'story in my head' technique lets you surface assumptions without making accusations
Healthy relationships require at least five positive interactions for every negative one
money_quotes: When a relationship has very little trust, you tend to interpret everything someone says in the worst possible light, or you may hear hostility, contempt, or dismissiveness where none exists.

The way trust gets rebuilt is by small, positive interactions. If you're in a trust hole, you can't hear them clearly, and they can't hear you (or your intent) clearly. So you have to bend over backwards to overcommunicate and overcompensate.
Firing off a reply while you are in fight-or-flight mode reliably leads to unintentional escalations.

"Assume positive intent" is a laudable goal, but in practice falls short. If every word someone says sounds accusatory or patronizing to you, what are you supposed to do with that advice? Just pretend you don't hear it, or tell yourself they mean well? That's not sustainable; your anger will only build up.
It may seem excessive and time consuming, but it will save you time and effort overall because you will have fewer miscommunications to debug.
posts.key_insight: Rebuilding trust requires deliberately overcommunicating positive intent and engineering frequent low-stakes positive interactions, because in a low-trust environment every neutral statement will be read as an attack.

posts.summary: Charity Majors argues that LLMs are nondeterministic black boxes that cannot be adequately tested or debugged using traditional software engineering techniques, making observability-driven development essential. She contends that the shift toward LLMs will force engineering teams to adopt modern practices like shipping early, observing production behavior, and tight feedback loops—practices that benefit all software development but that most teams have been slow to adopt.
key_points: LLMs produce nondeterministic outputs that break traditional testing and debugging assumptions
With ML, you must start with production to generate your tests, inverting the normal software development flow
Observability-driven development—shipping sooner, observing results, feeding back into development—is the only viable approach for LLM-based software
Generative AI makes writing code easy but understanding, operating, and maintaining it harder than ever
LLMs may serve as a Trojan Horse that forces engineering teams to finally adopt modern development best practices
money_quotes: LLMs are black boxes that produce nondeterministic outputs and cannot be debugged or tested using traditional software engineering techniques.
With software, you typically start with tests and graduate to production. With ML, you have to start with production to generate your tests.
In the past, the way most of us learned to understand software was by writing it.
The days when you could predict how your system would behave simply by reading lines of code are long, long gone.
LLMs might ultimately be the Trojan Horse that drags software engineering teams into the modern era of development best practices.
posts.key_insight: LLMs' inherent nondeterminism will force the entire software industry to adopt observability-driven development and production-first feedback loops—modern practices that most teams have resisted despite knowing they should embrace them.

posts.summary: Charity Majors argues that while the tech industry has rightly created alternatives to management as the only career path, engineering managers remain critically important as force multipliers. She makes the unconventional case that the best reason to try management isn't career advancement but personal growth — management forces you to develop foundational life skills like self-regulation, self-awareness, hard conversations, and boundary-setting that improve all your relationships.
key_points: The industry has evolved: staff+ engineering tracks now exist, so people no longer need to become managers for career progression or compensation
Good engineering managers are force multipliers — the biggest obstacles to productivity are organizational, not technical
Management forces you to develop deep interpersonal skills (self-regulation, self-awareness, boundary-setting, hard conversations) that transfer to all of life
Work is actually an ideal sandbox for practicing life skills because the social contract is more explicit and the stakes are lower than personal relationships
Technical skills decay but people skills are persistent and increase your optionality for life
money_quotes: Work is always about two things: what you put out into the world, and who you become while doing it.
A team with a good engineering manager will build circles around a team without one. The larger or more complex the org or the product, and the faster you want to move, the more true this is.

Work is actually kind of an ideal sandbox for life skills, because the social contract is more explicit. These are structured relationships, with rules and conventions and expectations, and your purpose for coming together is clear.
Managers don't make all the decisions, but they do ensure the decisions get made.
Technical skills do decay and become obsolete, particularly language fluency, but people skills do not. Once you have built these muscles, you will carry them with you for life.
posts.key_insight: The most compelling reason to try engineering management isn't career advancement but that it forces you to develop foundational interpersonal skills — self-regulation, self-awareness, hard conversations, boundary-setting — that make you better at all of life's relationships.

posts.summary: Charity argues that engineering managers are essential even at startups because hierarchy is a property of self-organizing systems that reduces coordination costs and information overload. She makes the case through systems theory (managers coordinate between teams and reduce context switching) and through pragmatism (most orgs have managers because the model is well-understood, and manager-free experiments have repeatedly failed).
key_points: Hierarchy is not intrinsically authoritarian — it's a property of self-organizing systems that emerges to reduce coordination costs and information overload
The 'socio' and 'technical' in sociotechnical systems are inseparable; most engineering work is glue work drawing on both skill sets
Engineering managers serve as useful abstractions — centralizing coordination into one role massively reduces context switching for engineers
Manager-free org experiments (holacracy at Medium/GitHub, etc.) have all been quietly abandoned due to chaos and poor execution
More lines of code does not equal more productivity — customers care about solving problems, not code output
money_quotes: When there is no explicit structure or hierarchy, the result is not freedom and egalitarianism, it's 'informal, unacknowledged, and unaccountable leadership.'
If you aren't making building the organization someone's number one job, it won't be anyone's number one job, which means it probably won't get done very well.
Your customers give zero fucks how many lines of code you write. They care about whether you are building the right things and solving problems that matter to them.
In natural hierarchies, we look up for purpose and down for function.
The nature of glue work is such that it too-easily expands to consume all available time and space. Constraints are good. Feeling a bit underresourced is good, and should be the norm.
posts.key_insight: Engineering managers are not overhead but a necessary abstraction layer in self-organizing systems — they reduce coordination costs, prevent information overload, and ensure that building the organization is someone's primary job rather than no one's.

posts.summary: The traditional 'three pillars' approach to observability (metrics, logs, traces) creates a cost crisis where organizations pay to store the same telemetry data multiple times across different tools, with costs growing several times faster than traffic while the value derived actually decreases. Charity argues that 'observability 2.0' built on arbitrarily-wide structured log events as a single source of truth fundamentally changes the cost-value equation, making telemetry more valuable as you add more data rather than less.
key_points: Observability 1.0's three pillars create a cost multiplier effect where every request's data is stored 3-10x across different tool categories
Metrics bills grow linearly (at best) with custom metrics and are incredibly opaque; unstructured logs get slower to search as volume increases
Observability 2.0 uses a single data source of wide structured events from which metrics, logs, and traces can all be derived
The most expensive hidden cost is not the tooling bill but the slowdown in engineering velocity caused by low visibility and low confidence
Dynamic sampling in observability 2.0 lets you capture 100% of interesting requests while controlling costs intelligently
money_quotes: For an alarming number of people, the value they get actually decreases as their bill goes up.
The most expensive part is the unseen costs inflicted on your engineering organization as development slows down and tech debt piles up, due to low visibility and thus low confidence.
The most important connective data in your system—connecting metrics with logs, and logs with traces—exists only in the heads of a few people.
The key difference between o11y 1.0 and o11y 2.0 has never been that either is cheap; it's that with observability 2.0, when your bill goes up, the value you derive from your telemetry goes up too.
Observability 2.0 is about being in conversation with your code, understanding each user's experience, and building the right things.
posts.key_insight: The observability cost crisis isn't primarily about the dollar amount on the invoice—it's that the traditional multi-pillar approach structurally guarantees that costs and value move in opposite directions, while a single-source-of-truth approach aligns them.

posts.summary: Charity Majors argues that the tech industry's belief that generative AI can replace junior engineers is dangerously misguided, because writing code is the easiest part of software engineering while the hard parts—operating, understanding, and managing complex sociotechnical systems—require human judgment built through years of apprenticeship. By refusing to hire and train junior engineers, companies are cannibalizing their own future pipeline of senior engineers.
key_points: Software engineering is an apprenticeship industry where most learning happens on the job over 7+ years, and no amount of AI-generated code changes that
Writing code is the easiest part of software engineering; the hard parts are operating, understanding, extending, and governing code over its entire lifecycle
AI-generated code cannot be trusted and always requires careful human review, making it more like fancy autocomplete than a replacement for engineers
The bottleneck isn't training junior engineers—it's companies refusing to give them their first jobs, treating them as costs to externalize rather than investments
High-performing teams require a diversity of experience levels; teams staffed entirely with senior engineers over-engineer solutions and compete for interesting work
money_quotes: Writing code is the easiest part of software engineering, and it's getting easier by the day. The hard parts are what you do with that code—operating it, understanding it, extending it, and governing it over its entire lifecycle.
By not hiring and training up junior engineers, we are cannibalizing our own future.
Hiring engineers is not a process of 'picking the best person for the job'. Hiring engineers is about composing teams.
The bottleneck is giving them their first jobs. The bottleneck consists of companies who see them as a cost to externalize, not an investment in their—the company's—future.

AI is not coming to solve all our problems and write all our code for us—and even if it was, it wouldn't matter. Writing code is but a sliver of what professional software engineers do, and arguably the easiest part.
posts.key_insight: Generative AI makes the already-easy part of software engineering (writing code) even easier, but does nothing for the hard parts (managing complex systems), so using it as justification to stop hiring junior engineers is a catastrophic mistake that will destroy the industry's pipeline of future senior talent.

posts.summary: Charity Majors argues that leaders must prioritize business success above all else, but that banning political speech at work is a cowardly cop-out that protects CEOs from uncomfortable feedback rather than protecting the mission. She advocates for a middle path: maintaining neutral, mission-focused workspaces while genuinely listening to employees and doing the hard work of navigating tensions rather than silencing them.
key_points: Business success must come first — it earns you the right to invest in culture and values
Good culture serves the business mission, not the other way around, and companies with terrible cultures win all the time
Bans on political speech at work are typically designed to protect CEOs from uncomfortable employee feedback, not to protect the mission
Neutral workspaces are valuable but must honor the paradox of tolerance — you cannot be equally tolerant of the marginalized and their oppressors
Leaders must do the hard work of navigating tensions, de-escalating drama, and genuinely listening to employees rather than silencing them
money_quotes: If people want to work at your company more than they want to use your product, that's a bad sign.

I believe that leaders who care about building a workplace culture rooted in dignity and respect have a responsibility to care even more about success in business. Let's show these motherfuckers how it's done.

If you don't spend time, money, attention, or political capital on it, you don't care about it, by definition. And it is a thousand times worse to claim you value something, and then demonstrate with your actions that you don't care, than to never claim it in the first place.

Just because you made a rule that people can't talk about the hard stuff, doesn't mean the hard stuff goes away. It mostly just serves to reinforce whatever power structures and inequities already exist in your company.
But you signed up for this, bro. It's part of the job, and you're getting paid a lot of money to do it. You don't get to just nope out when the going gets rough.
posts.key_insight: Banning political speech at work is not principled neutrality — it's leaders using their power to avoid the hard, uncomfortable work of actually leading through conflict and listening to their employees.

posts.summary: Charity Majors argues that observability tooling needs a version bump: 'Observability 1.0' (metrics, logs, traces as separate pillars) is fundamentally incompatible with 'Observability 2.0' (wide, structured log events as a single source of truth). She contends that metrics-based tools are increasingly expensive and inadequate for understanding complex modern systems, while structured event-based approaches offer superior debugging, cost efficiency, and developer experience.
key_points: The 'three pillars' (metrics, logs, traces) model is Observability 1.0 — structurally the same pre-aggregated metrics approach from a decade ago
Observability 2.0 stores data as arbitrarily-wide structured log events with a single source of truth, enabling read-time aggregation and infinite cardinality
O11y 1.0 is about operating code (dashboards, paging, MTTR); O11y 2.0 is about developing code (fast feedback loops, observability-driven development)
Metrics-based tools become more expensive and less valuable as systems grow more complex — the wrong tool for the job
The biggest psychological barrier to adopting O11y 2.0 is teams thinking they need to master O11y 1.0 first, which gets things exactly backwards
money_quotes: You cannot simultaneously store your data across both multiple pillars and a single source of truth. Incompatible. Breaking change. O11y 1.0, meet O11y 2.0.
Your job as a developer isn't done until you know it's working in production. Deploying to production is the beginning of gaining confidence in your code, not the denouement.
Metrics are a great tool for cheaply summarizing vast quantities of data. They are not equipped to help you introspect or understand complex systems. You will go broke and go mad if you try.
Teams are beating themselves up trying to master an archaic, unmasterable set of technical tradeoffs based on data types from the 80s. It's an unwinnable war.
Datadog and Prometheus are the last, best metrics-backed tools that will ever be built. You can't catch up to them or beat them at that; no one can. Do something different.
posts.key_insight: The shift from metrics-based observability to wide structured log events isn't an incremental improvement but a breaking change that unlocks fundamentally different — and vastly simpler — ways of understanding, debugging, and developing complex software systems.

posts.summary: Charity Majors argues that companies should build fair, transparent compensation systems and invest in ongoing career development rather than relying on heroic counter-offers when employees threaten to leave. She contends that healthy turnover is natural, that managers should proactively identify superstars and single points of failure, and that exceptions to compensation frameworks—while occasionally necessary—compound into cultural damage and cynicism.
key_points: Stay at your job as long as it fulfills your career priorities, but don't stay out of guilt or fear
Managers should proactively ensure roles remain great opportunities and be honest when they're not
Some employee turnover is natural and healthy—zero attrition can signal cult-like dysfunction
Companies should distinguish between superstars (retain out of joy) and SPOFs (retain out of fear), and have plans for both
Fair compensation requires structural work: pay bands, calibrations, equity audits, and refusing to reward brinksmanship
Counter-offers and exceptions to comp frameworks impose compounding costs to credibility and culture
money_quotes: The harder your employer is affirmatively working to do right by you, the fewer heroics they will be willing or able to do to retain you.
If the company's future depends irrevocably on the continued employment of any individual person, the company's leaders are fucking up, full stop.
If superstars are the people you want to keep out of joy, SPOFs can be the people you need to keep out of fear.
Any time you make an exception to keep someone, you risk breaking your commitments to everyone else.
People work at jobs for money, but not only money.
posts.key_insight: Sustainable employee retention comes from building fair, transparent systems for compensation and career development—not from heroic counter-offers that reward brinksmanship and erode organizational trust.

posts.summary: The fundamental distinction between observability 1.0 and 2.0 is not a marketing buzzword but a technical one: 1.0 scatters telemetry across multiple pillars, tools, and formats (many sources of truth), while 2.0 stores everything as wide structured log events in a single source of truth. Everything else—better developer workflows, sustainable cost models, high-cardinality exploration—flows as a consequence from this one architectural difference.
key_points: Observability 1.0 forces decisions at write time about how data will be used, siloing different data types into different tools
Observability 2.0 stores telemetry once as arbitrarily-wide structured log events and defers decisions to read time
The cost model of 1.0 is unsustainable because you pay to store data repeatedly across every pillar and tool
Three forces making 2.0 inevitable: system complexity explosion, brutal 1.0 cost models, and developers who've experienced 2.0 refusing to go back
Beware vendors claiming 'unified observability' when they really mean unified billing or visualization over multiple separate data stores
money_quotes: Observability 1.0 is a dinner knife; 2.0 is a scalpel.
Poor observability is the dark matter of engineering teams. It's why everything we do feels so incredibly, grindingly slow, for no apparent reason.
The idea that developing your code and operating your code are two different practices that can be done by two different people is no longer tenable.
If you're trying to clear a path through all the sales collateral and marketing technobabble, you only need to ask one question: how many times is your data going to be stored?
Once you've tried developing with observability 2.0, you can't go back.
posts.key_insight: The entire observability 1.0 vs 2.0 distinction reduces to one technical question—whether your telemetry is stored once as wide structured events (single source of truth) or scattered across multiple pillars and tools—and every other advantage of 2.0 flows as a consequence of that single architectural choice.

posts.summary: Charity Majors dissects Brian Chesky's 'founder mode' philosophy and hiring advice, acknowledging solid principles buried within but sharply criticizing his complete lack of accountability for Airbnb's organizational failures, his contemptuous treatment of employees, and the dangerous CEO-centric mythology that Silicon Valley continues to propagate. She extracts actionable lessons on hiring, leadership presence, and organizational efficiency while arguing that there are many valid architectural approaches to running a company.
key_points: Chesky's 'founder mode' narrative places all blame on employees while taking zero responsibility for the organizational dysfunction he created as CEO over 13 years
The advice to 'hire great people and get out of their way' is incomplete, but Chesky's alternative of routing everything through the CEO is just one valid architecture among many
Hypergrowth culture creates terrible organizational habits by solving every problem with more headcount, and the correction is brutal when efficiency finally matters
Hire people for their unique strengths, not their lack of weaknesses, and hire the 'right' people rather than the 'best' people
The Fundamental Attribution Error pervades Silicon Valley's cult of the founder, overemphasizing individual agency while ignoring the systems and conditions that drive behavior
money_quotes: But Brian Chesky created the fucking problems, by being bad at running the business! There is actually no shame in this! He is right: being a CEO is fucking hard. It does not come naturally. Nobody is born good at it.

I would PREFER the enduring legacy of both 'founder mode' and Brian Chesky's 'The Art of Hiring' to be one that moves the industry forward in material ways, and not one that further entrenches the Silicon Valley cult of the founder, Great Man of History, 10x engineer Lone Ranger superhero John Galt type bullshit that has dogged our heels for decades.

You want to hire the right people to join your team. Not the 'best' people. The right people. The fact that someone isn't a superstar employee for this company, this product, this team, at this stage, doesn't mean they might not be a superstar employee for someone else.
The team, the culture, the sociotechnical systems you hire them into are going to exert a gravitational pull over all of the people you hire. Systems drive behavior.
Doing great things with a small team is always something to brag about.
posts.key_insight: The 'founder mode' philosophy contains valid leadership principles about staying engaged with details and hiring for strengths, but its dangerous packaging as CEO-centric mythology ignores that organizational dysfunction is a systems problem driven by leadership choices, not proof that everyone else is mediocre.

posts.summary: Charity Majors pushes back against the 'Observability 3.0' framing proposed by Hazel Weakly, arguing that the critical advances described are already part of the Observability 2.0 generational shift already underway. She contends that the real measure of success isn't non-engineering functions interrogating engineering data, but rather engineers learning to integrate business data into their own telemetry and describe their work in the language of the business.
key_points: Semantic versioning as a framing device is overused and easily co-opted, but Observability 2.0 describes a real, backwards-incompatible generational shift from three pillars to wide structured events
New observability startups have stopped looking like cheaper Datadogs and started looking like cheaper Honeycombs, validating the architectural shift
Good product marketing simplifies strong technical differentiators; bad product marketing starts with justification and works backwards
Engineering is the laggard compared to business functions that have had columnar stores and flexible querying for decades
The end of ZIRP is forcing engineering leaders to justify their decisions in business terms, which requires integrating business data into engineering telemetry
money_quotes: This is not a branding exercise in search of technological justification, it's a technical sea change reaching for clarification in the market.
The success of observability 2.0 will be measured by how well engineering teams can understand their decisions and describe what they do in the language of the business.

It's a very 'the cobbler's children have no shoes' kind of vibe, that we're still over here warring over cardinality limits and pre-defined metrics and trying to wrestle them into understanding our massively, sprawlingly complex systems.

Twenty years of free money has done poorly by us as engineering leaders. The end of the ZIRP era is the best thing that could have happened to us. It's time to get our house in order and sit at the big table.

Every truly interesting question we ask as engineers is some combination or intersection of business data + application data. We do no one any favors by chopping them up and siloing them off into different tools and data stores, for consumption by different teams.
posts.key_insight: The real observability revolution isn't about non-engineering teams accessing telemetry data — it's about engineering teams finally integrating business context into their own tooling so they can speak the language of the business and earn a seat at the executive table.

posts.summary: Charity Majors argues that corporate DEI programs were always ineffective symbolic gestures, but the ideals underlying them — inclusion, equity, and counteracting bias — are essential to building genuinely meritocratic, high-performing teams. She contends that real cultural change came from hearts and minds being changed through storytelling and persuasion in the 2010s, not from corporate mandates, and that the best way to spread these values is to build successful companies that embody them.
key_points: Corporate DEI programs were largely performative and ineffective, but the backlash against them threatens genuinely important ideals
The 'seesaw fallacy' that DEI and high performance are opposites ignores that companies are systems that develop talent
True meritocracy requires actively counteracting bias — simply replicating society's existing biases is the opposite of merit-based
The real cultural shift in tech came from grassroots persuasion and consciousness-raising on social media in the 2010s, not corporate programs
When DEI advocates gained formal power post-2020, the shift from persuasion to mandates and speech policing triggered backlash
Diverse teams are a genuine competitive advantage, especially for recruiting senior talent who have their pick of roles
money_quotes: The problem isn't the fact that companies talk about their values, it's that they treat it like a branding exercise instead of an accountability mechanism.

That's how you build a real fucking meritocracy. You start with 'do not tolerate the things that kneecap your employees in their pursuit of excellence', and ESPECIALLY not the things that subject them to the compounding tax of being targeted for who they are.
There's no shame in having biases — we all have them. The shame is in making other people pay the price for your unexamined life.
If you want your values and ideals to spread throughout the industry, the most impactful thing you can possibly do is win.
When I look at the long list of companies who say they are rolling back mentions to DEI internally, I don't get that depressed. I see a long list of companies who never really meant it anyway.
posts.key_insight: True meritocracy demands active inclusion and bias counteraction — not corporate DEI theater — and the most powerful way to spread these values is to build winning companies that authentically embody them.

posts.summary: Charity Majors responds to Matt Klein's claim that bitdrift's ring buffer model deserves the 'Observability 3.0' label, while reflecting on the industry's shift from multiple pillars to unified storage models. She argues that while Klein raises valid points about cost control, his mobile-first approach of not sending telemetry by default doesn't translate well to complex, multi-tenant distributed systems that require real-time debugging. The industry is moving toward a data lake model with telemetry pipelines, tiered storage, and tail-based sampling as the next evolution of cost control.
key_points: Every observability startup founded after 2021 uses unified storage with columnar databases, while pre-2021 startups used multiple pillars
The multiple pillars model forces you to delete your most valuable data (context and detail) to control costs, while unified storage lets you delete your least valuable data via tail sampling
Ring buffer storage on mobile devices makes sense for isolated debugging but fails for real-time debugging of concurrent multi-tenant systems
Telemetry pipelines represent the slider between cheap write-time aggregation and expensive read-time aggregation of raw events
The long-term goal is unifying not just engineering telemetry but product analytics, business forecasting, and marketing data into a single source of truth
money_quotes: The fatal flaw of the multiple pillars model is that your levers of control consist of deleting your most valuable data: context and detail.
When it comes to developer tooling, cost matters, but it is rarely the most important thing or the most valuable thing. The most important things are workflows and cognitive carrying costs.

Data is made valuable by context. When you store it densely packed together — systems, app, product, marketing, sales — and derive insights from a single source of truth, how much faster might we move?
If we just say 'everything is observability', it robs engineers of the language they need to make smart decisions about instrumentation, telemetry and tools choices.
posts.key_insight: The observability industry is converging on unified storage models with telemetry pipelines and tail-based sampling as cost control levers, but the real value lies in workflows and context, not just reducing spend.

posts.summary: Charity Majors reflects on how Twitter was essential to both Honeycomb's growth and her personal creative process, serving as a dopamine source for her undiagnosed ADHD. With social media fragmented and her old writing workflow broken, she commits to a three-month experiment of writing shorter, faster blog posts weekly — learning to ship without the crutch of Twitter threads or deadline-driven panic.
key_points: Twitter conversations directly shaped Honeycomb's product strategy by revealing observability was an 'everybody problem'
Undiagnosed ADHD drove unhealthy coping mechanisms including sleep deprivation and Twitter-fueled dopamine hits
The fragmentation of social media has destroyed the combative tech discourse she thrived in
Epic Twitter threads that took enormous effort have vanished, while blog posts endured
She's committing to writing one sub-1000-word blog post per week to relearn scoping and shipping
money_quotes: In retrospect, I now realize that I was doing things like 'outbounding' and 'product marketing' and 'category creation', but at the time it felt more like oxygen.
I picked fights, I argued. I was combative and aggressive in public, and I loved it. I regret some of it now; I burned some good relationships, and I burned out my adrenal glands.
Most of those epic threads I put so much time and energy into crafting have vanished into the ether. The few that I bothered to convert into essay format are the only ones that have endured.
For most of my adult life, I've relied on hard deadlines and panic to drive projects to completion, or to determine the scope of a piece.

When I look back over things I have written, I feel like I can see myself growing up, my mental health improving, I'm getting better at taking the long view, being more empathetic, being less reactive.
posts.key_insight: The writing that endures is the writing you ship in durable form, not the writing that feeds the dopamine cycle of social media — and learning to ship without those crutches requires deliberately embracing imperfection.

posts.summary: Charity Majors reflects on how she and her college-dropout peers in early Silicon Valley developed an unfounded contempt for CS degree holders, using cherry-picked evidence to justify their own paths. She draws a broader lesson about how people in tech construct self-serving heuristics to validate their belonging, and argues for humility given the enormous role of luck and timing in tech careers.
key_points: Early career Silicon Valley dropouts developed genuine contempt for CS graduates, viewing degrees as evidence of poor judgment
Self-serving heuristics about talent are pervasive in tech and tend to reinforce existing power structures
Tech workers are in the top 0.0001% of career outcomes and should recognize the role of luck alongside hard work
The window when tech was an escalator to the middle class for dropouts and misfits has closed
money_quotes: Ever since then, I've met wave after wave of people in this industry who are convinced they know how to sift "good" talent from "bad" via easily detected heuristics. They're mostly bullshit.
I have retained an abiding skepticism of heuristics that serve to reinforce existing power structures, or ones that just so happen to overlap with the background of the holder of said heuristics.
I think I worked really fucking hard and I got really fucking lucky. Both can be true. Some of the smartest kids I grew up with are now pumping gas or dead.

There was a window of time in the 2000s when tech was an escalator to the middle class for a whole generation of weirdos, dropouts and liberal arts misfits. That window has been closed for a while now.
posts.key_insight: The heuristics tech workers use to judge talent are usually self-serving rationalizations that reinforce their own backgrounds, and the honest response to career success in tech is humility about how much luck and timing mattered.

posts.summary: Charity Majors argues against mandating pronoun sharing at work while supporting the normalization and safety of voluntary disclosure. Drawing from her own experience as a queer person with complicated feelings about gender identity, she explains how forced pronoun sharing can backfire by pressuring people who are questioning or transitioning, priming gender bias, and eliminating useful signal about genuine allyship.
key_points: Making pronoun sharing safe and normalized is good, but mandating it is counterproductive
Forced pronoun disclosure pressures people who are questioning, transitioning, or not yet out
Research suggests priming people to think of gender first can activate bias about competence
Mandates flatten useful social signal about who is genuinely a safe ally
Rules are easier to enforce than winning hearts and minds, but backlash is real
money_quotes: The strongest feeling I have about my gender is apathy/discomfort/irritation. Maybe that will change someday, maybe it won't, but I resent being forced to pick a side and make some kind of public declaration when I'm just trying to do my goddamn job.
Mandating it means we can't look around the room and determine who might be friendly or safe, while also escalating resentment that bigots hold towards us.

How much easier it is to make and enforce rules, if you're in a position with the power to do so, than to win hearts and minds. Rules always have edge cases and unintended consequences, and the backlash effect is real. People don't like being told what to do.
Research shows that priming someone to think of you as a woman first and foremost generally leads them to think of you as being less technical, less authoritative, even less competent.
posts.key_insight: Well-intentioned pronoun mandates at work can backfire by pressuring vulnerable people, activating gender bias, and destroying the very social signals that help queer people identify genuine allies.

posts.summary: Charity Majors argues that you typically know within the first week whether a new job is right for you, and that gut feeling rarely changes over time. She emphasizes this is especially true for managers, since alignment with the company is the core of the management job in a way it isn't for ICs. She advises trusting early impressions and learning from bad fits rather than waiting for things to improve.
key_points: First impressions of a new job are remarkably reliable — you usually know within the first week
Companies tend to drift along their current trajectory absent dramatic, painful action
Managers cannot phone it in the way ICs can because alignment IS the management job
A leadership team promoted entirely from within can be a yellow flag for insularity
Bad job experiences are valuable because they inoculate you against repeating the same mistake
money_quotes: Zero. You should give it 0 time. You already know, and you've known for a long time; it's not gonna change.
An IC can wall themselves off emotionally and still do acceptable work, but I'm not sure a manager can do the same.
It's not healthy to know you walk around every day fucking over one of your primary stakeholders, whether it's the company OR your teammates.
Companies can and do change dramatically. But absent dramatic action, which can be quite painful, they tend to drift along their current trajectory.
The engineer contributes their labor and output to move the company forward, but the manager contributes their emotional and relational selves — their humanity — to serve the cause.
posts.key_insight: Trust your early gut feeling about a new job — especially as a manager, where alignment with the company isn't optional but is the fundamental nature of the work itself.

posts.summary: Charity Majors argues that the tech industry's obsession with '10x engineers' is misguided because individual engineers don't own software—teams do. She makes the case that truly great engineering organizations are ones where normal, workaday engineers can consistently ship code and have business impact, rather than orgs that depend on a few brilliant individuals. The path to high performance is building better sociotechnical systems (fast deploys, observability, guard rails) that enable normal people to do great work.
key_points: Individual productivity measurements are flawed because skills are context-dependent and non-transferable across domains
Teams own software, not individuals—the smallest unit of software ownership is the engineering team
Great engineering orgs enable normal engineers to move fast and have impact, rather than requiring brilliance to get anything done
Deploy speed is the central feedback loop—short deploy cycles reduce cognitive load and coordination costs
Companies overindex on hiring pre-minted talent rather than building environments that develop engineers
Diverse, mixed-level teams are more resilient than monocultures of senior engineers
money_quotes: The smallest unit of software ownership and delivery is the engineering team.

Any asshole can build an org where the most experienced, brilliant engineers in the world can build product and make progress. That is not hard. And putting all the spotlight on individual ability has a way of letting your leaders off the hook for doing their jobs.
Great engineers are made, not born.
When your systems are designed to be used by normal engineers, all that excess brilliance they have can get poured into the product itself, instead of wasting it on navigating the system itself.
Software engineering isn't about writing lots of lines of code, it's about solving business problems using technology.
posts.key_insight: The mark of a great engineering organization isn't hiring the most brilliant individuals, but building sociotechnical systems where normal engineers can consistently ship, learn, and move the business forward.

posts.summary: Charity Majors reflects on her unconventional path from a fundamentalist homeschooled upbringing in rural Idaho to becoming a tech company founder, arguing that work can be a source of liberation, meaning, and personal growth rather than mere exploitation. She makes the case that people with strong ethics should pursue business and institutional power rather than ceding it to those without principles, and that the second half of a career should be oriented around purpose and building things of enduring value.
key_points: Left home at 15 from a fundamentalist homeschooled background in Idaho, put herself through college, and built a career in tech
Work has been a source of liberation, growth, and community throughout her life, not just a paycheck
Starting and running Honeycomb forced her to confront the gap between ideological ideals and business reality
People with ethics and ideals need to pursue business and institutional power rather than abandoning those arenas to exploitative actors
The second half of a 40-year career should be oriented around purpose, legacy, and building institutions worth belonging to
money_quotes: Creation is harder than critique — so, so, so much harder. And reality resists easy answers.
The most quietly radical, rebellious thing I can possibly do is to be an institutionalist, someone who builds instead of performatively tearing it all down.
There's nothing sinister about wanting power; what matters is what you do with it. Power, like capitalism, is a tool, and can be bent to powerful ends both good and evil.
If we prematurely abandon tech to its most exploitative elements, we guarantee its fate.
I worked a lot harder on the farm than I ever have in front of a keyboard, and got a hell of a lot less for my efforts.
posts.key_insight: People with strong ethics should actively pursue power and build institutions rather than ceding business and technology to those without principles, because creation and institutional stewardship — not critique — is what brings ideals to life.

posts.summary: Charity Majors resurrects a lost blog post from her Parse days documenting the two-year rewrite of Parse's API from Ruby on Rails to Go. The migration was driven by Rails' one-process-per-request model failing to scale during hockey-stick growth, and the team used production traffic shadowing to catch undocumented Rails behaviors. The experience of diffing individual API responses field-by-field during the migration directly inspired what would become Honeycomb's approach to observability.
key_points: Rails' one-process-per-request model couldn't scale past 200 API servers for 3000 req/s, with 20-minute deploys
The team evaluated EventMachine, JRuby, C++, C#, and Go before choosing Go for its lightweight goroutines and strong MongoDB driver
Production traffic shadowing — running requests against both Ruby and Go servers and diffing responses — was critical to catching undocumented Rails behaviors
The rewrite improved reliability by an order of magnitude, reduced the API server pool by 90%, and cut deploy time from 30 minutes to 3 minutes
The experience of using Scuba to compare individual responses during migration planted the seed for Honeycomb's observability approach
money_quotes: Rails HTTP processing is built on a philosophy of 'be liberal in what you accept'. So developers end up inadvertently sending API requests that are undocumented or even non-RFC compliant … but Rails middleware cleans them up and handles it fine.
Once you've used a tool like that, you're hooked.. you can't possibly go back to metrics and aggregates. The rest, as they say, is history.

We started looking ahead to when Parse would 10x its size, and realized that the one-process-per-request model just wouldn't scale. We had to move to an async model that was fundamentally different from the Rails way.
Our reliability improved by an order of magnitude. More importantly, our API is not getting more and more fragile as we spin up more databases and backing services.
// single geo query, don't do anything. stupid and does not make sense but ruby does it. Changing this will break a lot of client tests. just be nice and fix it here.
posts.key_insight: The grueling process of migrating Parse's API from Ruby to Go — and the production traffic diffing tools required to do it safely — directly seeded the observability philosophy that became Honeycomb.

posts.summary: Charity Majors announces she's been working on the second edition of 'Observability Engineering' and is adding a new section on 'Observability Governance.' She's asking for help from experienced software buyers because she lacks personal experience purchasing enterprise software at scale.
key_points: The second edition of 'Observability Engineering' has been in progress since April/May 2025
The observability landscape has changed dramatically since the first edition (2019-2022), with 'observability engineering teams' now widespread
A new 'Observability Governance' section covers staffing, org structure, tool purchasing, cost management, and executive buy-in
Charity openly admits she's never been a large-scale enterprise software buyer and is crowdsourcing expertise
money_quotes: Do you know, the phrase 'observability engineering teams' doesn't even occur in the first edition of the book? Try and search — it can't be found!
These days, observability engineering teams are everywhere.
I've never really bought software. Not like this. I've never even worked at a truly large, software-buying enterprise tech company.
I just want to gather up some good advice on buying software and funnel it back out to good engineers.
posts.key_insight: The observability field has matured so rapidly that even its leading practitioners need to crowdsource expertise on the governance, purchasing, and organizational challenges that now define how companies adopt observability at scale.

posts.summary: Charity Majors asks her audience for help answering specific questions about observability tooling for the second edition of her book, covering migrations, cost management, observability teams, and build-vs-buy decisions. She frames this as a follow-up to an earlier call for advice on software buying, which she argues is one of the highest-leverage activities a senior engineer can perform.
key_points: Vendor engineering is one of the highest leverage activities for staff+ engineers
Software buying decisions affect every engineer and every customer in the organization
Managing observability costs across the three pillars model remains a major challenge
Staff+ engineers can develop executive-level skills through vendor management while staying as individual contributors
The chapter on buying software is shaping up to be a highlight of the book's second edition
money_quotes: I've come to believe that vendor engineering is one of the subtlest and most powerful practical applications of deep subject matter expertise, and some of the highest leverage work an engineer can do.
How often do you get to make decisions that leverage the labor of hundreds or thousands of engineers per year, for fractions of pennies on the dollar?
It is one of the only ways a staff+ engineer can acquire and develop executive-level communication, strategy, and execution skills while remaining an individual contributor.
I watched him alter the trajectory of multiple other companies, none of which he was working for, buying from, or formally connected to in any way — in the space of a few conversations.
posts.key_insight: Vendor engineering — the art of evaluating, buying, and managing software tools — is an underrated, high-leverage skill that lets staff+ engineers develop executive-level strategic thinking while remaining individual contributors.

posts.summary: Charity Majors argues that the 'three pillars of observability' (metrics, logs, traces) is a marketing construct, not a technical one, used by vendors to justify selling siloed products at inflated prices. She advocates for a unified storage model (o11y 2.0) where all telemetry signals are stored together in one database, enabling seamless zoom-in/zoom-out debugging instead of bunny-hopping between disconnected tools. The piece also addresses misconceptions about OpenTelemetry reinforcing the pillars model, arguing it actually unifies signals through shared distributed context.
key_points: The 'pillars' framing is a marketing term that keeps engineers trapped in outdated mental models and paying for siloed tooling
The unified storage model stores all signals once in a single database, preserving context and relationships
The pillars model causes massive data duplication - the same information stored in metrics, logs, and traces formatted differently
OpenTelemetry actually treats everything like one big wide structured event log under the hood, despite vendor implementations reinforcing three-pillar silos
Most engineers who think they need profiling actually need better distributed tracing
money_quotes: There are no pillars. I think the pillars are a fucking lie, dude. I think the language of pillars does a lot of work to keep good engineers trapped inside a mental model from the 1980s, paying outrageous sums of money for tooling that can't keep up with the chaos and complexity of modern systems.

When a vendor starts talking about pillars, it means they are gearing up to sell you something: another type of signal, siloed off from all the other signals you send them. Your cost multiplier is about to increment again.
You are literally storing the same information in your metrics TSDB as you are in your logs and your traces, just formatted differently. This is insanely expensive.
No more bunny hopping around from pillar to pillar, guessing and copy-pasting IDs and crossing your fingers. Instead, it works more like the zoom function on PDFs or Google maps.
Instead, it's just a blob of json. Structured data. If you think it might be interesting to you someday, you dump it in, and if not, you don't. That's all. Cognitive load drops way down.
posts.key_insight: The 'pillars of observability' is a vendor marketing construct that justifies expensive siloed tooling; the technically superior approach is unified storage where all telemetry signals live together, enabling seamless debugging through zoom-in/zoom-out rather than hopping between disconnected data stores.

posts.summary: Charity Majors argues that the term 'observability' has been co-opted by vendors and executives who use it to describe basic monitoring tools, while the actual problem observability was meant to solve — understanding service quality from each individual customer's perspective — remains unaddressed by most 'observability' products. She coins the term 'o11ywashing' (analogous to 'cloudwashing') to describe this phenomenon and argues Honeycomb needs to better communicate observability's true value to executives, not just engineers.
key_points: Mainstream tech executives now use 'observability' to mean basic monitoring (up/down, metrics, logs, traces) while believing that understanding per-customer service quality is an unsolved problem requiring custom tooling
The original definition of observability — combining app, business, and system telemetry to understand quality from each customer's perspective — has been lost to marketing co-option
Legacy vendors and new entrants alike are 'o11ywashing' their products because the observability market is too lucrative to resist
The solution requires speaking to engineering executives in terms of business outcomes rather than technical details like cardinality and dimensionality
Observability should be understood as a systems problem rather than an operational one — it's about disrupting software doom spirals, not just firefighting
money_quotes: If your 'observability' tooling doesn't help you understand the quality of your product from the customer's perspective, EACH customer's perspective, it isn't fucking observability. It's just monitoring dressed up in marketing dollars.
This guy is literally describing the original definition of observability, and he doesn't even know it. He doesn't know it so hard that he went and built his own thing.
Observability is a systems problem. It's the most powerful lever in your arsenal when it comes to disrupting software doom spirals and turning them into positive feedback loops.

When Christine and I started Honeycomb, nearly ten years ago, we were innocent, doe-eyed engineers who truly believed on some level that if we just explained the technical details of cardinality and dimensionality clearly and patiently enough to the world, enough times, the consequences to the business would become obvious to everyone involved.
Companies are dumping more and more and more money into their so-called observability tools, and not getting any closer to a solution.
posts.key_insight: The term 'observability' has been hollowed out by vendors and executives to mean basic monitoring, and fixing this requires communicating observability's true value — understanding per-customer service quality as a systems problem — directly to engineering executives in business terms, not just to engineers in technical ones.

posts.summary: Charity Majors announces she's moving her blog from WordPress to Substack after a decade, citing WordPress friction and Substack's vibrant tech writing ecosystem. She notes she's been working on the second edition of 'Observability Engineering' and has accumulated ideas she's eager to publish once the drafts are done.
key_points: Moving blog from WordPress to Substack after 10 years due to publishing friction
Working on second edition of 'Observability Engineering' book
Substack has become the home of vibrant longform tech writing
Preserving old charity.wtf URLs to avoid breaking links
money_quotes: I despise WP (who doesn't?), and there's so much friction in getting a post out that I just don't do it.
It's clear that to the extent that there is a vibrant ecosystem of tech writers in longform, it's on substack.

I have learned SO MUCH in the writing process. As soon as these rough drafts have all been turned in, I will be streaming my thoughts out via substack. They are burning a hole in my brain the longer I hold them in.
WordPress may be a piece of shit, but the community I've found here has been anything but.
posts.key_insight: Publishing friction kills writing output, and platform migration — even after a decade — is worth it to remove barriers between ideas and audience.

posts.summary: Charity Majors explains her decision to move from WordPress to Substack despite criticism about the platform's content moderation policies. She argues that she's been neglecting a core part of her job as Honeycomb's CTO by going dark on social media, and needs a lower-friction platform to re-engage with the mainstream tech community. She acknowledges the ethical concerns but concludes Substack is far from being a platform that solely exists to support extremism.
key_points: Charity has been increasingly absent from social media and considers this an abdication of her professional responsibilities as CTO
The software world is changing fast with AI, and thought leaders need to be actively engaging with the community, not just publishing occasionally
WordPress felt too heavyweight and was creating friction that prevented regular posting
She researched the ethical concerns around Substack thoroughly before deciding to stay
She offers mitigations like not accepting paid subscriptions and encouraging RSS to minimize Substack's benefit
money_quotes: I have a job to do, and I haven't been doing it.
It is not enough to have interesting ideas and say them once, or write them down in a single book. You need to be out there mixing it up with the community every day, or at least every week.

How many times did we define observability or high cardinality or the sins of aggregation? Cool. How many times have we talked about the ways that AI has made the honeycomb vision technologically realizable for the first time? Uh, less, by an order of thousands.
Substack may not make all the decisions I would make, but I feel like it is a light dove grey, all things considered.
Everyone has to decide which battles are theirs to fight. This one is not mine.
posts.key_insight: Sometimes the most responsible professional choice is to prioritize reaching your audience over platform purity, especially when silence means failing to do your job.

posts.summary: Charity Majors argues that 2025 marked the tipping point where AI shifted from experimental satellite technology to mainstream foundational tech in developer tools, drawing a direct parallel to how cloud computing made the same transition around 2010. She acknowledges the bubble and hype but insists that froth doesn't disprove underlying value, and urges skeptics to engage rather than dismiss.
key_points: AI in developer tools has crossed the threshold from experimental to foundational, mirroring cloud's transition circa 2010
Professional skeptics like SREs need to engage with AI so their critiques are grounded in expertise rather than dismissal from the sidelines
Every major technology wave comes with a bubble, but the existence of hype doesn't negate real underlying value
The shift happened faster than expected — even Charity's own position evolved significantly in just eight months
money_quotes: But the existence of froth doesn't disprove the existence of value.
It doesn't mean there isn't a bubble. Of COURSE there's a fucking bubble. Cloud was a bubble. The internet was a bubble. Every massive new driver of innovation has come with its own frothy hype wave.

Their realism and attention to risk is more important than ever, but in order for their critique to be relevant and accurate and be heard, it has to be grounded in expertise and knowledge. Nobody cares about the person outside taking potshots.
This year was for AI what 2010 was for the cloud: the year when AI stopped being satellite, experimental tech and started being the mainstream, foundational technology.
posts.key_insight: AI has crossed the same inflection point cloud computing did around 2010 — it's no longer experimental but foundational — and skeptics must engage with it substantively rather than dismiss it as hype.

posts.summary: Charity Majors clarifies her stance on Friday deploy freezes, arguing they're a pragmatic workaround rather than a virtue to be celebrated. She offers two practical alternatives: freeze merges instead of deploys to prevent dangerous change buildup, and keep running the deploy pipeline without new code to avoid breakage from prolonged inaction.
key_points: Deploy freezes are a reasonable workaround when teams lack confidence in catching problems before customers do, but shouldn't be treated as a moral stance
Freezing deploys while continuing to merge code creates a dangerous snowdrift of untested changes that explode on January 5th
The real freeze should be on merges, not deploys — merging and deploying should feel like one atomic action
Systems that deploy frequently will likely break if you stop deploying for two weeks due to undetected issues like memory leaks
Keep running the deploy process daily even during quiet periods, just don't ship new code
money_quotes: The one thing that does get my knickers in a twist is when people adopt a holier-than-thou posture towards their Friday deploy freezes. Like they're doing it because they Care About People and it's the Right Thing To Do and some sort of grand moral gesture. Dude, it's a fucking hack. Just admit it.
All these changes build up like a snowdrift over a pile of grenades. You aren't going to find the grenades til you plow into the snowdrift on January 5th, and then you'll find them with your face.
People always remember the outages and instability that we trigger with our actions. We tend to forget about the outages and instability we trigger with our inaction.
If you want to freeze deploys, freeze merges. Let people work on other things. I assure you, there is plenty of other valuable work to be done.
posts.key_insight: Deploy freezes are a practical hack for teams lacking observability confidence, not a virtue — and if you must freeze, freeze merges instead of deploys to avoid a dangerous January pile-up of untested changes.

posts.summary: Charity Majors argues that the software industry has wrongly turned 'operations' into a dirty word, when ops is actually a vital separation of concerns — not a synonym for toil or a label for people who can't code. She traces this cultural rot partly to Google's SRE movement conflating 'writes code' with 'good' and 'does things by hand' with 'bad,' and calls for reclaiming operations as a term of pride to improve actual operational outcomes.
key_points: Operations is a separation of concerns (reliability/efficiency vs. features/revenue), not a skill hierarchy
Google's SRE framing inadvertently created a dev-good/ops-bad binary that degraded the profession
Infrastructure engineering is often harder than product engineering — ops absorbs the toughest problems to make product development simple
Platform engineering is functionally ops with better product thinking applied
Renaming ops teams doesn't improve operational outcomes; naming it honestly and building pride does
money_quotes: Operations is not a dirty word, a synonym for toil, or a title for people who can't write code. May those who shit on ops get the operational outcomes they deserve.
The difference between dev and ops is not about whether or not you can write code. Dude, it's 2026: everyone writes software.

Cognitive bandwidth is the scarcest resource in any engineering org, and you want as much of that as possible going towards things that move the business materially forward, instead of wrestling with the messy underbelly.
You don't make operational outcomes magically better by renaming the team 'DevOps' or 'SRE' or anything else. You make it better by naming it and claiming it for what it is.
The closer you get to laying bits down on disk, the more conservative (and afraid) you should be. The closer you get to user interaction, the more okay it is to get experimental.
posts.key_insight: The software industry's cultural contempt for 'operations' is both cause and consequence of poor operational outcomes — reclaiming ops as a term of pride is a prerequisite for building operational excellence.

posts.summary: Charity Majors announces the near-completion of the second edition of 'Observability Engineering,' which is almost twice as long as the first despite Martin Fowler's advice to make it shorter. The new edition features 90% new material, a clearer mission focused on software engineers, and contributions from a diverse group of industry practitioners, reflecting how much the observability landscape has changed since 2018.
key_points: The second edition of Observability Engineering is 90% new material and nearly twice the length of the first edition
The first edition suffered from a shifting market definition of observability during its 3.5-year writing process
The second edition has a clearer target audience (software engineers) and covers OpenTelemetry, AI, and modern observability practices
A diverse roster of guest contributors adds chapters on instrumentation, mobile observability, LLMs, CI/CD pipelines, and organizational transformation
A new section on 'Observability Governance' addresses cost containment, tool migrations, and platform engineering concerns
money_quotes: When we started the book in 2018, Honeycomb was the only observability company, and our definition of observability—high cardinality, high dimensionality, explorability—was the only definition. By the time the book came out in 2021, everyone was rebranding their products as observability, Gartner had waded into the fray.. it was a mess.
Most companies still don't have real observability. And they don't know it.
The integrations game is over, and OpenTelemetry has won.
I am excited and incredibly grateful for the opportunity to take a second whack at this book in the era of AI.
posts.key_insight: The observability landscape has transformed so dramatically since 2018—with OpenTelemetry winning, AI reshaping workflows, and the market co-opting the term—that a near-complete rewrite of the foundational book was necessary to reflect what observability actually means and requires today.

posts.summary: Charity Majors describes how she had to scrap and rewrite an entire section of the second edition of Observability Engineering after realizing she was writing tactical advice for teams operating in a strategic vacuum. Feedback from readers revealed that the real problems weren't technical implementation challenges but deeply dysfunctional organizational buying processes and a lack of shared understanding between engineering and leadership about what observability even is and why it matters.
key_points: Crowdsourced advice from readers revealed that observability challenges are primarily organizational and strategic, not technical
The rewritten section targets technical decision-makers (CTOs, VPs, distinguished engineers) and uses systems thinking language instead of overloaded technical jargon
Organizations are now limited not by delivery speed but by how quickly they can validate and learn from what they delivered
Observability is foundational to getting returns on AI investments, making it more relevant than ever despite some leaders deprioritizing it
money_quotes: I was writing tactical advice for teams who were surviving in a strategic vacuum.
Organizations used to be limited by the speed of delivery; now they are limited by how swiftly they can validate and understand what they delivered.
Your ability to get any returns on your investments into AI will be limited by how swiftly you can validate your changes and learn from them. Another word for this is 'OBSERVABILITY'.
Observability is the signal that connects the dots to make a feedback loop; no observability, no loop.
As Tolstoy once wrote, 'Happy teams are all alike; every fucked up team is fucked up in its own precious way.'
posts.key_insight: The biggest barrier to effective observability isn't technical implementation but the lack of shared strategic alignment between engineering teams and organizational leadership about what problem they're actually trying to solve.
